title: The Effects of Pensions and Retirement Policies on Retirement in Higher Education
context: This paper studies retirement decisions of tenured male arts-and-sciences faculty at 26 high-quality private U.S. colleges (COFHE) using employment observed as of December 31, 1975 and retirements during 1973–78, with pension plan details reported in 1979 (and 1989 for simulations). Institutional variation includes mandatory retirement ages (65–70), normal and early-retirement eligibility, pension accruals and early-retirement supplements, and Social Security rules. The authors estimate a structural life-cycle retirement model and use Monte Carlo simulations to predict effects of raising/eliminating mandatory retirement and offering early-retirement incentives.

unit_of_analysis: retirement decisions of tenured male arts-and-sciences faculty

title: Is Inequality Harmful for Growth? Theory and Evidence
context: Combining a politico‑economic growth model with empirical tests, the paper examines whether initial income inequality reduces subsequent per‑capita GDP growth. It uses two complementary data sets: a historical panel of nine now‑developed countries divided into 20‑year episodes covering primarily 1830–1970, and a post‑war cross‑section of about 67 countries over 1960–85. Units are country (or country‑period) observations; key covariates are pre‑period inequality (top‑20% share or top20/bottom40 ratio), schooling, franchise restrictions, and initial GDP per capita, exploiting cross‑country and over‑time variation in institutions and redistribution.

title: The Evolution of Buyout Pricing and Financial Structure
context: This paper studies 124 large U.S. management leveraged buyouts (MBOs) completed 1980–1989. Using transaction-level data from SEC proxy/14D filings, Mergerstat, CRSP and Compustat, the authors analyze firm-level pricing, projected cash flows, and capital-structure terms (bank term loans and revolvers, subordinated public/private debt, deferred-interest instruments, principal schedules) within the 1980s LBO/junk-bond market and evolving bank loan-sales and interest-rate environment to assess how valuation and financing changed over the decade.

title: Do Gasoline Prices Respond Asymmetrically to Crude Oil Price Changes?
context: This empirical study examines asymmetric transmission of crude oil price changes to gasoline prices using weekly and semi‑monthly data from January 1986–December 1990. It covers U.S. markets east of the Rocky Mountains (WTI crude, Gulf Coast/New York spot gasoline, city terminal branded/unbranded prices averaged across ~17 cities, and retail unleaded regular self‑service averages from 42 cities). Units are market prices at different stages of the refinery–terminal–retail distribution chain. The paper evaluates inventory‑cost and imperfect‑competition explanations using error‑correction/time‑series analysis.

region_bucket: U.S. markets east of the Rocky Mountains
unit_of_analysis: Units are market prices at different stages of the refinery–terminal–retail distribution chain

title: Reshaping Adolescents' Gender Attitudes: Evidence from a School-Based Experiment in India
context: This randomized evaluation studies cohorts of students who were in grades 6–7 at baseline (2013–14) in government secondary schools in four districts of Haryana, India. Breakthrough implemented a 27‑session, school-based gender‑equality curriculum from April 2014 to October 2016; the authors randomized 314 schools (150 treatment) and surveyed ~14,800 students (and a 40% parent subsample) at baseline, a first endline in late 2016–early 2017 and a second endline in 2019 to measure impacts on gender attitudes, aspirations, and self‑reported behaviors.

title: Judicial Checks and Balances
context: This paper studies 71 non-transition countries (sample based on Maddex 1995), relating constitutional provisions — judicial independence (tenure of supreme and administrative judges, case law) and constitutional review (rigidity, judicial review) — to mid-1990s measures of economic and political freedom (property rights c.1997, business entry procedures, employment regulation, government bank ownership c.1995, democracy and human/political rights mid-1990s). Units are countries; identification leverages persistent constitutional variation, legal origins, former-colony subsamples, and controls for latitude, ethnolinguistic fractionalization, and GDP per capita.

title: Fetal Exposure to Toxic Releases and Infant Health
context: Using U.S. data from roughly 1988–1999, the paper matches the EPA’s Toxic Release Inventory (TRI) to national Vital Statistics Natality and Mortality files to estimate effects of prenatal exposure to airborne toxic releases on gestation, birth weight, and infant mortality. Analyses aggregate January (and Jan–Mar for rare outcomes) births to identified U.S. counties (covering ~75% of births), exploit within-county year-to-year variation in reported fugitive versus stack air emissions, and compare chemicals labeled as developmental toxicants to other chemicals.

title: On the Nature of Capital Adjustment Costs
context: This paper analyzes a balanced panel of about 7,000 U.S. manufacturing plants continuously operating from 1972–1988 (Longitudinal Research Database). The unit of observation is the plant-year with detailed real gross expenditures and retirements used to construct capital stocks and investment rates. The authors estimate plant-level profit functions, decompose aggregate and idiosyncratic profitability shocks, and structurally recover convex and nonconvex adjustment costs (including irreversibility) by matching simulated policy-function moments to reduced-form investment–profitability regressions.

title: The Bidder's Curse
outcome: Fraction of auctions ending above fixed price
context: The paper studies bidder behavior on U.S. eBay by comparing completed auction prices to simultaneous “Buy-It-Now” fixed prices for identical items. Primary microdata include all Cashflow 101 auctions (Feb–Sep 2004) and a broader cross-section of auctions sampled in Feb–May 2007. Units are individual auctions, bids, bidders, and item listings on eBay’s modified second-price platform; observed institutional features include continuous fixed-price listings on the same webpage, shipping costs, and seller feedback scores. The design exploits simultaneous availability of fixed prices to test for systematic overbidding.

title: Interfirm Relationships and Business Performance
context: A large-scale field experiment in Nanchang, China (2013–2015) studied owner-managed micro, small and medium enterprises established within the prior three years. From 2,800 interested firms researchers randomized 1,480 into treatment groups of ten managers who held monthly self-organized meetings for one year (Aug 2013–mid-2014) and 1,320 into a no-meetings control, with baseline (2013), midline (2014) and endline (2015) surveys. The program (run with the local CIIT) also randomized group composition, information shocks about financial products, and one-time cross-group meetings.

title: The Effects of Urban Concentration on Economic Growth
context: This paper uses a cross-country panel of about 80–100 countries over 1960–1995 (five-year intervals) to estimate how national urban concentration—measured by primacy (the largest metro area’s share of national urban population)—affects economic growth. title: What Do Parents Value in Education? An Empirical Investigation of Parents' Revealed Preferences for Teachers
context: This paper uses administrative and survey data from a mid-size public school district in the western United States to analyze parents' revealed preferences for elementary school teachers. It studies parental teacher requests collected in the 2003–04 and 2004–05 school years (with a principal survey in February 2003), covering 11 of 13 elementary schools and 251 teacher-year observations. School assignment is determined by residence (no open enrollment); parents may optionally request or avoid specific teachers. Requests are linked to teacher demographics, principal evaluations, and classroom value-added measures.

title: PPP Strikes Back: Aggregation and the Real Exchange Rate
context: This empirical study uses monthly Eurostat sectoral CPI price indices (19 goods across ≈13 European countries; about 222 country–sector series) and aggregate CPI real exchange rates versus the US dollar over roughly 1975–1996. Units are country–sector monthly relative prices and country-level aggregate real exchange rates. The paper examines how cross‑sector heterogeneity in autoregressive dynamics biases pooled and aggregate estimates of real‑exchange‑rate persistence (half‑lives), comparing fixed‑effects, Anderson‑Hsiao/Arellano‑Bond dynamic panels, aggregate ARs and a Random Coefficient Model, with Monte‑Carlo simulations; motivation is the PPP puzzle, arbitrage frictions and nominal rigidities.

title: What's News in Business Cycles
context: This paper estimates a structural DSGE model for the postwar U.S. economy using quarterly data from 1955:Q1–2006:Q4. The unit is the aggregate U.S. economy (per-capita GDP, consumption, investment, government spending, the relative price of investment, and hours worked). The model (an RBC framework with habit formation, investment adjustment costs, and variable capacity utilization) is driven by stationary and permanent TFP shocks, investment-specific shocks, and government spending shocks; Bayesian estimation separates anticipated from unanticipated innovations.

title: Labor in the Boardroom
context: This triplet analyzes firms' labor productivity (value added per worker) in the paper's 1994 reform setting: stock corporations incorporated 1989–1999 are compared in two‑year cohorts around the August 10, 1994 cutoff. Value added per worker is measured using Bureau van Dĳk (BvD) value added divided by BvD employment, and the productivity analysis relies on the Orbis‑ADIAB/BvD component (coverage begins in 2006). Units are firms (stock corporations), with LLCs as an additional control group.

title: The Power of Suggestion: Inertia in 401(k) Participation and Savings Behavior
context: Analyzes employee-level 401(k) behavior at a large U.S. Fortune 500 health insurance company using administrative data from June 1997–June 1999 around an April 1, 1998 policy change. Units are individual employees grouped into hiring cohorts (OLD, WINDOW, NEW). The firm switched from one-year eligibility with affirmative enrollment to immediate eligibility with automatic enrollment (default 3% contribution into a money-market fund, opt-out possible) while keeping economic plan features (match, fund menu) unchanged, exploiting this quasi-experiment to estimate effects on participation, contributions, and allocations.

title: Ambiguous Business Cycles
context: This paper estimates a medium-scale DSGE model for the United States using quarterly macro data (1984Q1–2010Q1) to quantify how time-varying ambiguity (agents’ changing confidence about productivity) drives business cycles. The unit of analysis is aggregate US macro variables (GDP, consumption, investment, hours, inflation, short-term rate, investment price). Theoretical setting features representative ambiguity-averse households (recursive multiple-priors) who treat neutral productivity innovations as ambiguous; ambiguity shocks enter as time-varying belief-set widths and are estimated in a Bayesian state-space framework.

title: Family Violence and Football: The Effect of Unexpected Emotional Cues on Violent Behavior
context: Using 1995–2006 NIBRS police-incident data matched to six U.S. NFL home-team markets, this analysis examines police-reported violence against children on Sundays during the regular NFL seasons. The unit is police-agency by Sunday counts of child-directed incidents. Expected game outcomes are benchmarked with Las Vegas point spreads, and regressions control for agency fixed effects, seasonality, weather, and local Nielsen viewership; actual game outcomes are treated as random conditional on the spread. Low baseline reporting rates limit statistical power.

title: Wages, Profits and Rent-Sharing
context: This paper analyzes US manufacturing wage determination using CPS March microdata for roughly 400,000 workers aggregated into 19 industry-by-year cells over 1964–1985. Industry-level profit and unemployment series (profit data available 1958–1985, aggregated to 2-digit SIC) are merged with cell means of individual characteristics to test rent-sharing versus competitive and contract models. Estimations use dynamic panel specifications with year dummies and both fixed- and random-effects to trace contemporaneous and lagged profit effects on mean wages.

title: Estimating Trade Flows: Trading Partners and Trading Volumes
context: Using bilateral trade data for 158 countries over 1970–1997 (with focused analyses for 1986 and the 1980s), the paper examines exporter–importer country-pair outcomes (whether a link exists and export volumes). It embeds a heterogeneous‑firms model with fixed and variable export costs to decompose effects of trade frictions (distance, tariffs, WTO/FTA membership, common language, borders, currency unions, entry regulation) into intensive (exports per exporter) and extensive (number of exporters) margins. Empirical identification exploits exporter/importer fixed effects, a Probit selection for trading links, Heckman corrections, and structural counterfactuals.

title: Economic Effects of Runs on Early 'Shadow Banks': Trust Companies and the Impact of the Panic of 1907
context: This paper studies the impact of runs on New York trust companies during the Panic of 1907 (using 1903–1912 firm-panel data with emphasis on Oct–Dec 1907) on NYSE-listed nonfinancial firms (industrials and railroads) and aggregate U.S. investment. Trust companies—early “shadow banks” outside the New York Clearing House with low reserves—suffered deposit runs after a failed United Copper corner involving prominent financiers. The authors use board interlocks to link firms to affected trusts and examine stock returns, accounting outcomes, and investment responses to the liquidity shock.

title: The Dynamic Behavior of the Real Exchange Rate in Sticky Price Models
context: This triplet refers to model-generated impulse responses in the paper’s two‑country Calvo sticky‑price framework (local‑currency pricing, staggered price setting, home‑bias preferences and Taylor‑rule monetary policy). In model simulations (Figure 6), a positive home monetary policy shock raises home consumption and, through higher demand, causes home CPI inflation to increase; both variables then return monotonically to steady state.

title: What Goods Do Countries Trade? A Quantitative Exploration of Ricardo's Ideas

title: Estimating Trade Flows: Trading Partners and Trading Volumes
context: Using bilateral trade data for 158 countries over 1970–1997 (with focused analyses for 1986 and the 1980s), the paper examines exporter–importer country-pair outcomes (whether a link exists and export volumes). Empirical identification exploits exporter/importer fixed effects, a Probit selection for trading links, Heckman corrections, and structural counterfactuals.

title: The Enduring Impact of the American Dust Bowl: Short and Long-run Adjustments to Environmental Catastrophe
context: This paper examines the economic consequences of the 1930s American Dust Bowl by comparing counties classified as low/medium/high erosion using Soil Conservation Service maps across 769 U.S. Plains counties (12 Plains states) in a county-level panel spanning 1910–1997. Using census and administrative data on land values, agricultural revenue, population, banks, and New Deal payments, it studies short-run (1930–1940) and long-run adjustments in agriculture, migration, and local industry during the Great Depression and New Deal conservation era.

title: An Account of Global Factor Trade
context: This study tests Heckscher-Ohlin-Vanek predictions using mid-1980s (circa 1985) input-output, trade, and endowment data for ten OECD countries (Australia, Canada, Denmark, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, Netherlands, United Kingdom, United States) and an aggregated Rest of World. The unit of observation is country-by-industry sectors and bilateral trade flows. The authors estimate country-specific Hicks‑neutral productivity, sectoral input coefficients, and gravity-based bilateral import demand to assess departures from factor-price equalization, specialization in tradables, and the role of trade costs in net factor service trade.

title: Dams

title: Competitive Equilibrium in Markets for Votes

title: Subways, Strikes, and Slowdowns: The Impacts of Public Transit on Traffic Congestion
context: This study analyzes the short-run impact of a 35-day Los Angeles County MTA strike (Oct 14–Nov 18, 2003) that halted bus and rail service. Using hourly loop-detector data on freeway speeds and minutes-per-mile delay across major Los Angeles freeways during weekday peak hours, the paper studies detector-by-hour outcomes to estimate how ceased transit service affected congestion. It combines a calibrated commuter-choice model (Los Angeles parameters) with a regression-discontinuity design using date as the running variable, detector fixed effects, and falsification/placebo tests against nearby counties and a 2004 placebo period.

unit_of_analysis: hourly loop-detector data on freeway speeds and minutes-per-mile delay

title: Estimating the Effect of Treatments Allocated by Randomized Waiting Lists.
context: This paper studies causal inference for oversubscribed treatments allocated by randomized waiting lists: applicants are randomly ranked within waitlists and offers are sequentially made until available seats are filled, in settings with capacity constraints and “non-takers.” The unit of observation is individual applicants across many waitlists (e.g., schools or training sites). The authors derive estimator properties, propose a doubly-reweighted ever-offer estimator, and illustrate empirically using Blattman & Annan’s randomized agricultural-training program for Liberian ex-combatants (70 waitlists).

region_bucket: Blattman & Annan’s randomized agricultural-training program for Liberian ex-combatants (70 waitlists)

title: Patent Publication and Innovation
context: The paper studies the effect of the American Inventors Protection Act (AIPA) of 1999—which required U.S. patent applications filed on or after Nov 29, 2000 to be published 18 months after filing—on innovation and knowledge diffusion. Using patent applications filed 1998–2003 across the U.S., the European Patent Office (and Japanese Patent Office) and firm-level R&D, the authors compare U.S. patents to “twin” foreign equivalents and exploit variation in pre-AIPA grant delays, opt-out rates, and firm technology exposure to estimate impacts on citations, citation speed, similarity, abandonments, claims, and R&D.

time_decade: required ... filed on or after Nov 29, 2000

title: Incumbent Behavior: Vote Seeking, Tax Setting and Yardstick Competition
context: Analyzes U.S. state-level tax changes and gubernatorial reelection outcomes in the 48 continental states from 1960–1989 (TAXSIM 1977–88 and Statistical Abstract 1960–89). Unit: state-year observations and governor reelection events. Exploits variation in changes in effective income tax liabilities and per-capita sales+income tax collections, with attention to neighboring states' tax changes, election timing, term limits, and demographic/economic controls; assumes voters use neighboring jurisdictions as benchmarks under asymmetric information about public-service cost shocks.

unit_of_analysis: Unit: state-year observations and governor reelection events

title: Foreign Direct Investment and Domestic Economic Activity
context: This study examines U.S. manufacturing multinational parent firms using confidential BEA benchmark-survey panel data for 1982, 1989, 1994 and 1999 (covering changes between 1982–1999). The unit of observation is U.S. parent firms and their foreign affiliates located across many host countries. The institutional background is the U.S. multinational tax and investment environment and debates over outbound FDI. Identification leverages variation in host-country GDP growth interacted with each firm’s lagged geographic distribution of foreign investment.

title: The Long-Run Effects of the Scramble for Africa
context: This empirical study evaluates long-run effects of the Scramble for Africa (late 19th-century colonial border drawing) by comparing historical ethnic homelands (Murdock 1959) that were split by modern borders to those that were not. The unit of observation is ethnic-group homelands across African countries. Main outcomes span georeferenced political violence (ACLED 1997–2013; UCDP GED 1989–2010), ethnic political status over the post‑independence era (EPR), and household-level welfare (DHS). Analyses exploit within-country comparisons with geographic controls and fixed effects.

unit_of_analysis: The unit of observation is ethnic-group homelands across African countries.

title: Beyond the Incidence of Training: Evidence from a National Employers Survey
context: This analysis uses the nationally representative Educational Quality of the Workforce National Employers Survey (EQW-NES) of U.S. establishments to examine how school and employer-provided training (human-capital investments) relate to workplace practices and establishment outcomes. The unit of observation is establishments across manufacturing and non-manufacturing sectors; the paper examines determinants and recipients of training, links between human and physical capital, variation by employer size, and the association between human-capital investments and establishment productivity.

title: Indirect Effects of Access to Finance
context: This paper reports a field experiment in Jiangxi province, China, around a new no-collateral SME loan introduced in 2013. The authors randomized monthly loan-officer outreach to 3,173 small and medium retail and service firms clustered in 78 local geographic “markets” (treatment intensity: 0%, 50%, 80%), collected baseline data in summer 2013 and followed firms in 2015, 2016 and a 2020 follow-up. The institutional setting features concentrated local retail markets, pervasive credit constraints, and bank-mediated loan approval with standardized terms.

title: Demand Analysis using Strategic Reports: An application to a school choice mechanism
context: Analyzes elementary school admissions in Cambridge, MA (Controlled Choice Plan) over 2004–2009, using individual kindergarten applicants (≈400/year, 13 schools) and submitted rank-order lists. title: Subsidy Policies and Insurance Demand
context: This estimate comes from the two-year (2010–2011) randomized field experiment in Jiangxi province on ~3,500 rice-farming households, focusing on the subset of households who paid for insurance in year 1 (non-free villages) or were willing to purchase in free villages. title: Vulnerability and Clientelism
context: This paper studies rural, water‑vulnerable households in Brazil’s semi‑arid Northeast during 2011–2013. It analyzes a randomized cistern construction program implemented from 2012 across 40 municipalities (425 neighborhood clusters, ~1,308 eligible households) and municipal‑level rainfall shocks (including the severe 2012 drought), using baseline (Oct–Dec 2011) and follow‑up household surveys (Nov–Dec 2012 and 2013) linked to 2012 electronic voting‑machine returns. The institutional setting features limited formal insurance/credit, common clientelist exchanges with local politicians, and compulsory municipal elections in Oct 2012.

title: Relative-Price Changes as Aggregate Supply Shocks
context: This paper analyzes U.S. postwar inflation from 1949–1989 by linking annual cross‑sectional distributions of four‑digit PPI industry price changes to aggregate PPI inflation. Working from a menu‑cost model of nominal rigidity, the authors argue that skewness in relative‑price changes (for example, large oil shocks) shifts the short‑run Phillips curve. Empirically they compute yearly moments (weighted and unweighted) of industry price changes, estimate AR(1) time‑series and Phillips‑curve regressions including detrended unemployment, and examine historical episodes.

title: Short and Long Run Externalities
context: Using U.S. manufacturing data for 450 four-digit industries from 1958–1986 (with a 1977 input–output matrix to construct interindustry weights), the paper analyzes how external economies transmit through customer (output-weighted) and supplier (input-weighted) linkages. Units are four-digit manufacturing industries; the study distinguishes short-run (fluctuation-oriented) effects from longer-run (growth-oriented) effects and conducts robustness checks for capacity utilization and alternative activity measures (value added vs. gross production). Identification exploits variation across industries and over time in the postwar U.S. manufacturing sector.

context: This triplet examines the randomized IRS pilot's effect of informational letters (mailed Nov 2016–Jan 2017) on two-year mortality (deaths in 2017–2018) among previously uninsured non-elderly adults ages 45–64 in the United States. title: The Welfare Effects of Encouraging Rural-Urban Migration
context: This paper studies rural–urban migration in northern Bangladesh using randomized migration-subsidy experiments conducted in the Rangpur region (notably a 2008 RCT with follow-ups and a larger 2014 replication). context: This paper studies Pennsylvania’s nursing home industry using resident-level MDS admissions and facility surveys and cost reports, primarily for 2000–2002 (with robustness to earlier years). title: How the Other Half Died: Immigration and Mortality in US Cities
context: Empirical study of U.S. cities from 1900–1937 (348 cities) estimating how the 1921 and 1924 national immigration quota acts changed city-level mortality. The authors construct city-level “missing immigrants” (quota exposure) from pre‑WWI migration trends and 1910 settlement patterns, and use annual cause-of-death counts (infectious, non-infectious, external) to assess spillovers of reduced immigration on urban public health, controlling for age structure, pandemic years, city and year fixed effects, and state-by-year shocks.

title: The Skill Complementarity of Broadband Internet

title: Relative-Price Changes as Aggregate Supply Shocks
context: This paper analyzes U.S. postwar inflation from 1949–1989 by linking annual cross‑sectional distributions of four‑digit PPI industry price changes to aggregate PPI inflation. The setting is a partially integrated urban housing market with matching frictions; the paper quantifies how heterogeneous search ranges shape inventory, turnover, prices, and responses to local construction. treatment: Broad searchers attracted to unstable segments outcome: Searchers per house"

title: Regional Redistribution Through the U.S. Mortgage Market
context: This paper analyzes U.S. mortgage markets using loan-level data from Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac (1999–2012, with focal analyses for 2001–2006 and 2007–2009/Great Recession) and private “prime jumbo” loans across metropolitan statistical areas. The units are individual mortgage loans and MSAs. The institutional setting contrasts government-sponsored enterprise (GSE) securitization—with an implicit federal backstop and a national conforming-loan limit—and privately securitized jumbo mortgages; the authors exploit regional lagged default, prepayment, and the conforming-loan threshold to study spatial redistribution via mortgage pricing.

title: Inattention and Switching Costs as Sources of Inertia in Medicare Part D
context: Using a 20% administrative sample of U.S. Medicare Part D claims (2007–2010), the paper analyzes annual plan‑choice decisions of individual beneficiaries (age 65+) enrolled in stand‑alone PDPs without low‑income subsidies. The institutional setting is the Medicare Part D market with ~50 private plans, standardized ANOC notices, and automatic re‑enrollment defaults during annual open enrollment. The authors exploit forced choosers (new enrollees or discontinued plans) and year‑to‑year plan changes to estimate a structural two‑stage attention-and-switching‑costs panel discrete‑choice model and simulate interventions.

unit_of_analysis: annual plan‑choice decisions of individual beneficiaries

title: Pricing and Welfare in Health Plan Choice
context: This empirical study analyzes employer-sponsored health plan choice using administrative data from 11 small-to-mid sized employers in a single metropolitan area in the western United States during 2004–2005 (3,683 employee-years; 6,603 enrollee-years). The intermediary offered menus from two insurers (an integrated delivery-system HMO and a broader-network HMO/PPO); insurers submitted first-year bids based on age/sex, the intermediary computed DxCG risk scores and transfers, and employers set employee contributions under institutional constraints limiting risk-rating of contributions.

title: Intra-Industry Foreign Direct Investment
context: This paper uses Dun & Bradstreet WorldBase establishment-level data (2004–2005) covering roughly 650,000 multinational subsidiaries across about 90 countries and 400 four-digit SIC industries to study FDI motives. The unit of observation is the establishment/plant, with regression analyses focused on manufacturing bilateral parent–subsidiary industry pairs. The authors classify subsidiaries as horizontal or vertical using the 1987 BEA input–output matrix and analyze determinants of intra-industry vertical FDI using cross-sectional Tobit regressions of subsidiary counts, sales, and employment on market, distance, skill and proximity measures.

title: The Effects of High-Skilled Immigration Policy on Firms: Evidence from Visa Lotteries
context: Using U.S. administrative data from USCIS H-1B lotteries (mainly FY2006–FY2007, with additional FY2008 and 2003–2008 analyses) matched to IRS firm-level tax records and USPTO patent data, the paper studies causal impacts of marginal H-1B visa allocations on U.S. firms (EINs). The institutional setting is the capped H-1B program (Regular and Advanced Degree Exemption) with lotteries when caps bind; outcomes include quarterly employment, patents, R&E tax credit claims, payroll per employee, and firm profits.

title: Gender Differences in Medical Evaluations: Evidence from Randomly Assigned Doctors
context: The paper analyzes independent medical evaluations for Texas workers’ compensation claimants with injuries occurring 2013–2017 (exams through 2017). The unit is injured workers/first independent medical exam; designated doctors (MD/DO/DC) are randomly assigned within claimant county and provider credential via an automated Texas Department of Insurance list. Designated doctors assess maximum medical improvement and determine subsequent cash disability benefits; the authors link randomized assignment to 12-month post-exam cash benefit outcomes to estimate effects of patient–doctor gender match.

title: Estimating Time Preferences from Convex Budgets
context: This paper reports a laboratory experiment run at UC San Diego in January 2009 on 97 undergraduate students (freshmen/sophomores living on campus). Subjects completed 45 convex time-budget (CTB) allocation decisions (varying start times t = 0,7,35 days and delays k = 35,70,98 days) converting tokens to monetary payments; payments were routed to campus mailboxes with procedures to equalize transaction costs and payment confidence. The setting is a controlled lab environment designed to identify individual time-preference parameters (discounting, present bias, utility curvature).

title: Relative-Price Changes as Aggregate Supply Shocks
context: This paper analyzes U.S. postwar inflation from 1949–1989 by linking annual cross‑sectional distributions of four‑digit PPI industry price changes to aggregate PPI inflation. Working from a menu‑cost model of nominal rigidity, the authors argue that skewness in relative‑price changes (for example, large oil shocks) shifts the short‑run Phillips curve. Empirically they compute yearly moments (weighted and unweighted) of industry price changes, estimate AR(1) time‑series and Phillips‑curve regressions including detrended unemployment, and examine historical episodes.

title: Wages, Profits and Rent-Sharing
context: This paper analyzes US manufacturing wage determination using CPS March microdata for roughly 400,000 workers aggregated into 19 industry-by-year cells over 1964–1985. Industry-level profit and unemployment series (profit data available 1958–1985, aggregated to 2-digit SIC) are merged with cell means of individual characteristics to test rent-sharing versus competitive and contract models. Estimations use dynamic panel specifications with year dummies and both fixed- and random-effects to trace contemporaneous and lagged profit effects on mean wages.

title: The Marginal Product of Capital
outcome: Heterogeneity in marginal product of capital
context: Cross-country analysis using a cross-section of about 53 countries circa 1996–2000 (Penn World Tables 6.1 for GDP/capital; World Bank wealth data ~2000; Bernanke & Gurkaynak capital shares). Unit of observation is the country. The authors assume competitive domestic goods and factor markets and Cobb–Douglas production, and they adjust for reproducible vs. natural capital and for domestic relative prices of investment goods. They perform structural counterfactual reallocations of the world reproducible-capital stock to assess deadweight losses from non‑equalized returns.

title: Missing Growth from Creative Destruction
context: The paper studies U.S. private nonfarm businesses from 1983–2013, using plant-level microdata (Longitudinal Business Database) and manufacturing subsamples to quantify measurement bias ('missing growth') from statistical imputation when products exit (creative destruction). It develops a growth model with entrant, incumbent, and variety innovation, then empirically infers innovation arrival rates and quality step sizes via a market-share accounting approach and an indirect-inference structural algorithm applied to establishment-level employment, payroll, entry and exit dynamics.

title: Are Idle Hands the Devil's Workshop? Incapacitation, Concentration and Juvenile Crime
context: This paper analyzes daily incident- and arrest-level crime data (NIBRS) for 29 U.S. jurisdictions during 1995–1999, merged with school-district calendars. The unit of observation is city-day counts of juvenile offenders; the setting is U.S. public school calendars (teacher in-service days, holidays, breaks, summer). The study exploits plausibly exogenous variation in in-service and idiosyncratic break days to estimate short-run effects of school being in session on juvenile property and violent crime, controlling for city*year*month and day-of-week effects and testing robustness with NCVS checks and weekly aggregation.

unit_of_analysis: The unit of observation is city-day counts of juvenile offenders

title: Growth in Cities
context: Using County Business Patterns employment, payroll, and establishment data for 170 U.S. Standard Metropolitan Areas, the paper examines growth of the top six 2‑digit industries in each city between 1956 and 1987 (≈1,016 city‑industry observations). It assesses how local specialization, competition (establishments per worker), and urban variety predict 30‑year employment and wage growth, controlling for initial employment and wages, southern location, and national industry trends, to test MAR, Porter, and Jacobs theories of knowledge spillovers.

title: Estimating Trade Flows: Trading Partners and Trading Volumes
context: Using bilateral trade data for 158 countries over 1970–1997 (with focused analyses for 1986 and the 1980s), the paper examines exporter–importer country-pair outcomes (whether a link exists and export volumes). It embeds a heterogeneous‑firms model with fixed and variable export costs to decompose effects of trade frictions (distance, tariffs, WTO/FTA membership, common language, borders, currency unions, entry regulation) into intensive (exports per exporter) and extensive (number of exporters) margins. Empirical identification exploits exporter/importer fixed effects, a Probit selection for trading links, Heckman corrections, and structural counterfactuals.

title: Aiding Conflict: The Impact of U.S. Food Aid on Civil War
context: This paper uses annual country‑year data for 134 non‑OECD countries from 1972–2006 to estimate how U.S. wheat food aid (PL‑480/USDA shipments) affects civil conflict incidence, onset and duration. Identification exploits exogenous year‑to‑year variation in U.S. wheat production (weather‑driven) interacting with each country's historical propensity to receive U.S. food aid, controlling for country fixed effects and region‑year shocks. Outcomes are civil and interstate armed conflict (UCDP/PRIO); analyses include panel regressions, IV 2SLS, and hazard models for conflict duration.

title: Cementing Relationships: Vertical Integration, Foreclosure, Productivity, and Prices
context: This paper studies U.S. cement (upstream) and ready-mixed concrete (downstream) plants and firms using Census of Manufactures microdata from 1963–1997, constructing market-year observations (CEAs and USGS/EAs). Units are individual plants and markets; analyses exploit variation in vertical integration, multi-plant firms, firm entry/exit, and changing antitrust enforcement regimes and local construction demand. The setting features high transport costs, localized markets, and policy shifts in FTC/DOJ scrutiny of vertical mergers, allowing causal assessment of integration’s effects on prices, quantities, productivity, and turnover.

title: The Economic Consequences of Increasing Sleep Among the Urban Poor
context: This paper reports a month-long field RCT in Chennai, India, with 452 low-income urban adults employed in a one-month paid data-entry job (8-day baseline then ~3-week treatment) to study causal effects of increased sleep. Interventions—cross-randomized—included night-sleep treatments (information, sleep-improving devices, and sometimes financial incentives) and workplace half-hour afternoon naps in a comfortable office; outcomes include actigraphy-measured night and nap sleep, labor supply, productivity, cognition, well-being, and economic preferences.

title: Testing for Altruism and Social Pressure in Charitable Giving
context: Field experiments conducted primarily April–October 2008 (with complementary surveys into 2009) in suburbs around Chicago contacting ~7,668 households via door-to-door solicitations for two charities (local La Rabida children’s hospital and an out-of-state charity). Households were randomized at the street/solicitor-day level to no notice, a one-day flyer announcing a one-hour visit, or a flyer with a do-not-disturb opt-out; complementary randomized survey treatments varied pay and duration. The setting tests social pressure versus altruism in unsolicited fundraising.

title: How Are SNAP Benefits Spent? Evidence from a Retail Panel
context: Using transaction-level retailer panel data from February 2006–December 2012 together with Rhode Island and national SNAP administrative records, the paper analyzes how monthly SNAP EBT benefits affect household grocery and nonfood spending. The setting is a large U.S. grocery chain’s loyalty-card customers (≈486,570 households across several states) and administrative SNAP data for Rhode Island. The analyses exploit institutional features of SNAP: monthly electronic benefit disbursements, certification/recertification cycles (many spells divisible by six months), and legislated benefit increases (2008 Farm Bill and April 2009 ARRA).

title: Cross-Country Technology Diffusion: The Case of Computers
context: The paper analyzes international diffusion of computer technology using country-year observations from roughly 1970–1990 (focus years 1970,1975,1980,1985,1990) for up to ~155 countries. Adoption is proxied by imports of computer equipment per worker (with robustness checks on non-exporting countries and UNIDO production+net imports (OCAM)). The setting is cross-country: country-level outcomes (per-worker adoption) are related to human capital, OECD vs non-OECD manufacturing trade, investment, sectoral composition, property-rights indices, government size, and regional/time effects.

unit_of_analysis: The setting is cross-country: country-level outcomes (per-worker adoption)

title: Bank Integration and State Business Cycles
context: This triplet focuses on U.S. state-level small-firm employment growth volatility using annual state-year observations over the deregulation era; small-firm employment (establishments with <20 employees) is drawn from County Business Patterns and the growth-rate series effectively covers 1978–1994. The paper links volatility to banking integration—the share of state bank assets owned by multi-state bank holding companies—exploiting staggered state-level interstate-banking deregulation and controlling for industry composition, and state and year fixed effects.

title: Disability Insurance Benefits and Labor Supply
context: This triplet evaluates social-welfare effects of the January 1987 increase in CPP disability benefits (≈36% relative to Quebec's QPP). Gruber uses elasticity estimates of labor‑force non‑participation (0.25–0.32) from April SCF data on men aged 45–59 (1985–89) and conducts simulations with a utilitarian CES social‑welfare function, two groups (workers and the disabled), tax financing of the benefit increase, and parameters for crowd‑out and risk aversion to assess net welfare gains.

title: Production Networks, Geography and Firm Performance
context: This paper studies how reduced person-to-person travel costs affect firm productivity and supplier links in Japan, exploiting the March 2004 Kyushu Shinkansen opening. Using exhaustive firm-level buyer–seller linkages from TSR (≈950,000 firms), firm balance-sheet panels (2000–2008) and network snapshots in 2005 and 2010, the authors analyze firms and municipality-pair linkages across Japanese localities and industries. The rail extension sharply cut passenger travel times (not freight), enabling more face-to-face meetings; identification compares firms near new stations to others over time and by industry input-intensity.

title: The Causes and Effects of Liability Reform: Some Empirical Evidence
context: This paper analyzes the effects and determinants of state-level tort liability reforms in the United States over 1969–1990. The authors compile a new panel of state tort statutes and judicial decisions matched to industry–state–year data (17 local industries plus manufacturing) from the BEA, and study how adoption of eight reforms (e.g., damage caps, contingency-fee limits, joint-and-several, comparative negligence, prejudgment interest) affected industry productivity and employment. Units are state-industry-year observations; authors exploit staggered cross-state adoption and use political/professional variables (lawyers/doctors per capita, party control, ADA scores) as instruments.

title: Estimating the Effect of Training on Employment and Unemployment Durations: Evidence From Experimental Data
context: Using the National Supported Work (NSW) Demonstration randomized experiment (volunteers randomized in 1976–77) across seven U.S. sites (Atlanta, Chicago, Hartford, Newark, New York City, Oakland, and Wisconsin locations), the paper analyzes post-baseline employment and unemployment spell durations for AFDC recipient women over roughly 26 months of semi-monthly follow-up. The program provided 9–18 months of subsidized work; eligibility required recent unemployment and volunteer enrollment, and random assignment ensures exogenous treatment variation.

Claimants are grouped by profiling-score bins (6–13, 14–15, 16, 17–19) and impacts on weeks of UI, dollars of benefits, and earnings are compared across these score groups.

context: This paper analyzes household-level panel data on maize production in rural Kenya (representative maize-growing areas, ~1,200 households across 22 districts) over 1996–2004 (using 1997–2004 waves), studying adoption of hybrid maize and fertilizer. title: Targeted Debt Relief and the Origins of Financial Distress: Experimental Evidence from Distressed Credit Card Borrowers
context: Between January 2005 and August 2006 Money Management International randomized 79,739 prospective clients across U.S. states into debt-management-plan (DMP) offers. Units are distressed credit‑card borrowers; treated individuals received larger immediate minimum‑payment reductions and delayed debt write‑downs negotiated with eleven card issuers, while controls received status‑quo DMP terms. The study links counselor records to PACER bankruptcy data, TransUnion credit reports, and SSA W‑2 tax records to measure repayment, bankruptcy, credit, employment, and 401(k) outcomes over roughly five years.

title: New Frontiers: The Origins and Content of New Work, 1940–2018
context: Analyzes the United States, 1940–2018, linking decadal Census/ACS microdata on occupations and industry write-ins to a constructed database of new job micro‑titles and to the full text of U.S. utility patents. title: Cost-Effective Policies to Reduce Vehicle Emissions
context: This paper uses US household-level data (Consumer Expenditure Survey, 1996–2001; 9,027 households) together with vehicle emissions data (CARB) to estimate a structural discrete–continuous demand system for vehicle ownership (0/1/2), vehicle type (car vs SUV), vehicle age, and vehicle-miles-traveled. The unit of analysis is the household and its vehicles. Simulated counterfactuals assess gasoline, distance, emissions, and vehicle “wear” taxes within the US vehicle emissions regulatory setting to quantify emissions reductions, consumer-surplus changes, and deadweight loss.

title: Parental Resources and College Attendance: Evidence from Lottery Wins
context: Using U.S. administrative data, the paper studies children (units: individuals/household members) whose parents won state lotteries between 2000 and 2013 to estimate how parental financial shocks affect college attendance. It links state W-2G lottery reports to federal tax filings, Form 1098-T college enrollment records, and FAFSA/financial-aid data, comparing wins of varying sizes and timing relative to expected high-school graduation across states and cohorts. Institutional features exploited include third-party W-2G reporting and FAFSA rules that treat near-term windfalls as income but earlier wealth differently.

title: Firm and Worker Dynamics in a Frictional Labor Market
context: This study develops and estimates a structural continuous-time random-matching model of frictional labor markets for the United States, calibrated and estimated to matched employer–employee microdata and aggregate U.S. moments. Units are multi-worker firms and individual workers; the model captures firm entry/exit, job creation/destruction, vacancy posting, and on-the-job search. Empirical exercises match cross-sectional firm-size, hiring and worker-flow moments and simulate counterfactuals including a Great Recession–type decline in firm entry (circa 2007–09) and shocks to match efficiency/discounting.

title: Implications of Skill-Biased Technological Change: International Evidence
context: This paper studies manufacturing labor markets across OECD and selected developing countries mainly during the 1970s–1990s (with focal windows like 1979–87 and 1979–90). title: News Shocks in Open Economies: Evidence from Giant Oil Discoveries
context: The paper uses a country-year panel (up to 130 countries, 1970–2012; baseline regressions 1980–2012), treating giant oil discovery announcements (NPV as percent of GDP) as plausibly exogenous news shocks with a typical 4–6 year lag and estimating dynamic responses via panel distributed-lag/event-study regressions with country and year fixed effects. For the real exchange rate specifically, the theoretical model omits a non-tradeable sector (so provides no formal prediction), and empirically the authors examine CPI- and GDP-deflator-based RERs, finding small, imprecise, statistically insignificant appreciation in the first five years after discovery.

title: The Effect of Macroeconomic Uncertainty on Firm Decisions
context: Using two survey waves in New Zealand (first wave June–Oct 2021, follow-up Nov 2021–Feb 2022), the authors survey ~4,000 firms (about 2,000 reinterviewed) employing ≥6 workers. They randomized information treatments that provided professional forecasters' mean GDP forecasts and/or the spread between optimistic and pessimistic forecasts to exogenously shift firms' macroeconomic first- and second-moment beliefs during the pandemic period. Outcomes include firms' planned and realized prices, employment, investment, sales, advertising, and decisions to adopt technologies or open facilities.

title: Micro Effects of Macro Announcements: Real-Time Price Discovery in Foreign Exchange
context: This empirical paper analyzes 5‑minute interbank spot exchange‑rate returns for the U.S. dollar versus the German mark, British pound, Japanese yen, Swiss franc and euro using Reuters quotes from January 1992–December 1998. Units are 5‑minute returns around scheduled U.S. and German macroeconomic announcements (including FOMC) paired with real‑time MMS survey forecasts and realized releases. The institutional setting is the global interbank FX market; analyses focus on immediate price discovery and volatility responses to announcement “surprises” across currencies and announcement types.

title: An Economic Analysis of a Drug-Selling Gang's Finances
context: This paper analyzes monthly financial and event data for a single street gang over a recent four‑year period (values reported in 1995 dollars) operating in an inner‑city neighborhood of a large U.S. industrial city. The unit of observation is the gang-month (monthly revenues, costs, wages, quantities/prices of crack sales) augmented by member-level counts of deaths, injuries, and arrests and indicators for gang wars and a turf-expansion episode. The institutional setting is an illicit crack market with hierarchical tribute payments, episodic violent conflict, and eventual leader arrests.

context: Using cross-country annual data (primarily 1961–2000, sample of ~58 countries aggregated into non‑overlapping decades), the paper links systemic financial risk—proxied by skewness of real bank‑credit growth and multiple crisis indexes—to per‑capita GDP growth and investment. The unit of analysis is countries (and country‑decades); key institutional contrasts are financial liberalization (de facto and de jure indexes) and contract enforceability (1984 law‑and‑order index). title: Do Doctors Practice Defensive Medicine?
context: The triplet concerns state-level malpractice liability reforms in the United States that directly reduce provider liability pressure, and their effect on mortality for elderly Medicare beneficiaries treated for serious heart disease in 1984, 1987, and 1990. Units are individual Medicare beneficiaries; mortality is assessed up to two and five years after reform adoption across states.

ideology_sensitivity: state-level malpractice liability reforms in the United States that directly reduce provider liability pressure
preferred_signs: state-level malpractice liability reforms in the United States that directly reduce provider liability pressure

title: Public Monopoly and Economic Efficiency: Evidence from the Pennsylvania Liquor Control Board's Entry Decisions
context: This paper studies Pennsylvania’s state-run retail monopoly for wine and spirits (the Pennsylvania Liquor Control Board) using store-level daily and weekly sales, wholesale costs, and geocoded store locations for 2005, linked to 2000 Census tract demographics. Units of observation include PLCB stores, Census tracts (consumers over 21), and store-week/day sales. The PLCB sets uniform retail prices via markup rules and controls entry and store locations; the authors estimate a structural spatial demand model and simulate profit- and welfare-maximizing store networks and counterfactual private-entry equilibria.

unit_of_analysis: Units of observation include PLCB stores, Census tracts (consumers over 21), and store-week/day sales.

title: Racial Diversity and Racial Policy Preferences: The Great Migration and Civil Rights
context: The paper examines the political effects of the Second Great Migration (circa 1940–1970) in the United States, focusing on non‑southern counties and congressional districts as units of analysis. It studies how northward and westward inflows of Black migrants from southern states reshaped local electorates, grassroots civil‑rights activism, media attention to southern racial violence, and legislators’ race‑related behavior during the era that produced the 1964–65 Civil and Voting Rights Acts. Key institutional context includes southern disenfranchisement, northern voting rights, and labor‑union dynamics.

context: This paper studies 3rd–7th grade students in the Brookline (MA) public schools using ITBS scores from 1994–2000 to measure how Boston’s METCO desegregation program — which buses predominantly Black students from Boston into suburban districts — altered class composition and peer achievement. The unit of observation is individual students and grade/school/year classroom cells; the setting features voluntary METCO placements, contractual class-size caps, and administrative assignment rules used to probe peer and class-size effects.

title: Transition to Clean Technology
context: This paper combines a micro-founded endogenous-growth model with firm-level estimation using U.S. energy-sector microdata from 1975–2004. It analyzes 1,576 innovative firms (6,228 firm-period observations) using Census LBD and Economic Census records, NSF R&D survey data, and USPTO patents to study firm R&D, patents, production, employment, entry/exit, and emissions. The structural model contrasts clean versus fossil-fuel (dirty) technologies, is calibrated to carbon-cycle dynamics, and evaluates policy counterfactuals (carbon taxes and clean-research subsidies) for production, innovation direction, and welfare.

title: A Rehabilitation of Monetary Policy in the 1950s
context: This paper examines U.S. monetary policy in the 1950s — primarily 1952–1958 after the 1951 Treasury–Fed Accord — and contrasts it with later eras (1964–1979, 1979–1987, 1987–2000). Using Federal Reserve FOMC minutes and quarterly macro data (federal funds rate, GDP deflator, real GDP), it assesses policymakers’ motives and actions and estimates a forward-looking policy rule to evaluate how the Fed adjusted nominal rates to expected inflation and output gaps.

title: The Regulation of Labor
context: This paper analyzes cross-country variation in labor-market regulation using detailed indices of employment law, collective-bargaining law, and social‑security law measured around 1997 for 85 countries. Units are countries; political controls use the fraction of years that executives and legislatures were left/center between 1975–1995 (and robustness to 1928–1995). The institutional context includes legal origin (common, French, German, Scandinavian, socialist), trade openness and political constraints. Main outcomes include unofficial-economy size, labor-force participation, unemployment (especially ages 20–24), and relative wages.

title: From Education to Democracy?
context: This triplet is estimated using the country-level panel (about 108 countries) over 1965–2000 at five-year intervals. Units are countries; democracy is measured primarily by the Freedom House index (robust to Polity and Przeworski measures). Income per capita (log GDP per capita) is included as a right-hand covariate (Table 3, cols.5–6); estimation exploits within-country variation with country fixed effects and dynamic panel methods (Arellano-Bond GMM), treating log GDP per capita as predetermined and instrumenting its first difference with lagged levels.

title: Profitability of Fertilizer: Experimental Evidence from Female Rice Farmers in Mali
context: In spring 2010 an RCT in Bougouni district, southern Mali randomly assigned 383 women rice farmers (one per household) to receive no, half, or full recommended fertilizer per hectare; follow-ups were conducted in August and December 2010. Rice is rainfed, broadcast-farmed on small, women-controlled plots; fertilizer is officially recommended but under-used despite government subsidies. The study evaluates how exogenous fertilizer grants affect input choices, output, and profits under typical (no intensive monitoring) extension conditions.

title: The FOMC versus the Staff:  Where Can Monetary Policymakers Add Value?
context: This paper analyzes forecasts and policy decisions of the U.S. Federal Reserve over 1979–2001 (forecast data through 2001; monetary-shock series through 1996). The unit of observation is twice-yearly FOMC-member and Board staff forecasts (Greenbooks and Monetary Policy Reports) and realized macro outcomes for inflation, unemployment, and real GDP growth. The institutional setting is the FOMC process in which members see staff forecasts and may revise forecasts after meetings; the paper examines whether policymakers’ forecast deviations predict subsequent federal funds rate surprises.

unit_of_analysis: The unit of observation is twice-yearly FOMC-member and Board staff forecasts (Greenbooks and Monetary Policy Reports) and realized macro outcomes

context: This paper evaluates a clustered randomized trial (2007–2017) in 460 rural communities across six sub-districts in south-central Bangladesh, studying unmarried adolescent girls aged 15–17 (and their households/communities). title: Consumption Risk and Expected Stock Returns

Within a (C‑)CAPM framework it contrasts contemporaneous and long‑horizon consumption betas (S quarters ahead) and examines consumption of stockholders using the Consumer Expenditure Survey. title: Private Returns to Public Office
context: Using mandatory sworn asset affidavits introduced beginning November 2003 under India’s transparency reforms, the paper examines net-asset growth of state legislative candidates who recontested across assembly elections in 24 Indian states between 2003 and 2011 (roughly five-year cycles). title: Growth in Cities
context: Using County Business Patterns data for 170 U.S. SMAs (1956–1987), the paper analyzes the top six 2‑digit industries per city (city‑industry units). Wage‑growth regressions are estimated on the subset of 834 city‑industry observations with exact payroll and employment data; wages are measured as payroll per employee and used (imperfectly) as a productivity proxy. Regressions control for initial employment and wages, southern location, and national industry trends; the outcome is 1956–1987 wage growth.

title: Do Banks Pass Through Credit Expansions to Consumers Who Want to Borrow?
context: Using monthly account-level data on credit cards issued by the eight largest U.S. banks from January 2008–December 2014, the paper studies how changes in banks’ cost of funds during the Great Recession affected consumer borrowing. Units are individual credit‑card accounts (merged to credit bureau records). It exploits discontinuities in credit limits at FICO score cutoffs to estimate causal effects of extra credit on card- and portfolio-level borrowing, and combines these RD moments with a model of optimal credit limits to infer banks’ marginal propensity to lend.

context: This paper evaluates long-term impacts of Project STAR, a randomized classroom-size and teacher-assignment experiment conducted in 79 Tennessee schools during 1985–1989 among a cohort born ~1979–1980. It links K–3 STAR test scores and randomized classroom/teacher assignments to administrative tax and 1098-T records from the late 1990s through 2007 to measure outcomes (college attendance, college quality, earnings at ages ~25–27, home ownership, retirement savings). Units are individual students randomized within schools to small (≈15) or regular (≈22) classes.

title: The Housing Boom and Bust: Model Meets Evidence
context: This paper studies the U.S. housing boom and bust around the Great Recession (roughly 1997–2015), combining household-level lifecycle heterogeneous-agent data and aggregate time series. The unit of analysis is households in an overlapping-generations setting interacting with competitive rental, construction, and mortgage intermediation markets. It calibrates a structural incomplete-markets model with long-term defaultable mortgages, HELOCs, rental markets, and shocks to productivity, housing finance (LTV, PTI, origination costs) and beliefs about future housing demand, and evaluates counterfactuals including large-scale mortgage principal forgiveness.

The unit is parents of NCLB-school students; the analysis compares spring 2004 choices to July 2004 choices after receiving NCLB information. Proximity is measured by presence of higher-scoring schools (e.g., within five miles) in the parent’s choice set.

title: Does Welfare Prevent Crime? The Criminal Justice Outcomes of Youth Removed From SSI
context: This paper estimates effects of losing Supplemental Security Income (SSI) at age 18 due to the 1996 federal welfare reform (PRWORA) medical‑review rule that applied adult eligibility criteria to children with 18th birthdays after August 22, 1996. title: Insurance Rationing and the Origins of Workers' Compensation
context: This triplet is estimated using household-level data from the 1917–1919 Bureau of Labor Statistics Cost-of-Living study (over 7,000 households) across U.S. states, exploiting cross-state variation in whether workers' compensation was in force. It focuses on early 20th-century U.S. labor markets where private accident insurance appeared rationed, and examines how state-level workers' compensation availability affected household precautionary saving.

title: Social Action, Private Choice, and Philanthropy: Understanding the Sources of Improvements in Black Schooling in Georgia, 1911-1960
context: This paper studies changes in Black schooling quality in Georgia from 1911–1960, using school-district–level measures (pupil–teacher ratios, teacher salaries, term length, certification, enrollments) and statewide comparisons across Southern states. Units include Georgia school districts and Southern states in event-history analysis. The institutional setting is segregated public schooling under Jim Crow, with state funding apportionment, northern philanthropy (Rosenwald, Jeanes, Slater), Great Migration, the 1926 equalization law, the Depression/WWII era, and NAACP litigation campaigns affecting teacher pay and school resources.

title: Subsidizing Health Insurance for Low-Income Adults: Evidence from Massachusetts
context: This paper studies Massachusetts' subsidized insurance exchange "CommCare" from 2009–2013, focusing on fiscal year 2011. It uses individual-level administrative enrollment, claims, and reported income (% of FPL) for non-elderly adults (ages 19–64) eligible between 135–300% of the federal poverty level who lacked employer or Medicaid coverage. CommCare's subsidy schedule produced discrete premium jumps at 150%, 200%, and 250% FPL (and an individual mandate with penalties); these institutional discontinuities identify causal effects of enrollee premiums on take-up, selection, and insurer costs.

title: Giffen Behavior: Theory and Evidence
context: Field experiment in 2006 (surveys April, Sept, Dec 2006; subsidies June–Oct 2006) across 11 county seats in two Chinese provinces (Hunan and Gansu). title: Using Mandated Speed Limits to Measure the Value of a Statistical Life
outcome: Hours required to travel a mile
context: Study examines the effect of states' adoption of a 65 mph maximum speed limit (the 1987 federal change that allowed states to raise rural interstate limits) on hours required to travel a mile (the reciprocal of average speed times vehicle miles traveled). Using state-by-roadtype-by-year panel data for 1982–93 and focusing on rural interstates (including 21 adopting states with complete post-1987 speed data), the paper fits the reduced-form equation (12b) on pooled years and finds about a 4% decrease in hours per mile on rural interstates after adoption, with no effect on urban interstates or rural arterials.

title: Patent Publication and Innovation
context: The paper studies the effect of the American Inventors Protection Act (AIPA) of 1999—which required U.S. patent applications filed on or after Nov 29, 2000 to be published 18 months after filing—on innovation and knowledge diffusion. title: Product Differentiation, Multi-product Firms and Estimating the Impact of Trade Liberalization on Productivity
context: This paper studies Belgian textile firms over 1994–2002, using matched plant‑level and product‑level data (308 firms, 2,990 firm‑product observations) to estimate how EU textile quota liberalization affected firm productivity. title: Firm Dynamics, Job Turnover, and Wage Distributions in an Open Economy
context: The paper analyzes Colombian manufacturing plants (establishments with ≥10 workers) using annual plant-level panel data primarily from 1981–1990 (pre-reform) and evaluates post-1990s outcomes following trade liberalization and 1991 labor-market reforms. It studies how reductions in trade frictions (tariffs and iceberg costs), falling firing costs (dismissal costs), and increased global integration affected firm dynamics, job turnover, unemployment, and cross-firm wage distributions via a calibrated dynamic general-equilibrium model.

title: Family Violence and Football: The Effect of Unexpected Emotional Cues on Violent Behavior
context: Using 1995–2006 NIBRS police-incident data matched to six U.S. NFL home-team markets, the paper studies family violence on Sundays during regular NFL seasons. It benchmarks unexpected game outcomes with Las Vegas point spreads and controls for agency fixed effects, weather, seasonality, and local Nielsen viewership. The design treats actual game outcomes as random conditional on the spread to estimate causal effects of upset wins/losses.

title: The Causes and Effects of Liability Reform: Some Empirical Evidence

title: The Damages and Distortions from Discrimination in the Rental Housing Market
context: This paper combines a large-scale randomized correspondence field experiment on a major online rental platform in five U.S. MSAs (Atlanta, Houston, Philadelphia, Cleveland, San Jose) with residential-location data (InfoUSA) on renter moves (2016–2018). title: Innovation and Foreign Ownership

title: Understanding Bank Runs:  The Importance of Depositor-Bank Relationships and Networks
context: This paper studies depositor behavior around a March 2001 cooperative-bank failure in Gujarat, India. Using minute-by-minute transaction records (Jan 2000–Jan 2002) for deposit and loan accounts at a nearby cooperative bank headquarters, the unit of analysis is individual deposit accounts/households. The institutional setting includes India’s cooperative-banking rules (introducer requirement), flat deposit insurance (Rs100,000), no ATMs, and a localized run after a large bank’s fraud-induced collapse (event window March 9–15, 2001). Analyses exploit variation in account balances, account age, loan linkages, neighborhood/introducer networks, and withdrawal timing.

title: Judicial Checks and Balances
context: This paper studies 71 non-transition countries (sample based on Maddex 1995), relating constitutional provisions — judicial independence (tenure of supreme and administrative judges, case law) and constitutional review (rigidity, judicial review) — to mid-1990s measures of economic and political freedom (property rights c.1997, business entry procedures, employment regulation, government bank ownership c.1995, democracy and human/political rights mid-1990s). Units are countries; identification leverages persistent constitutional variation, legal origins, former-colony subsamples, and controls for latitude, ethnolinguistic fractionalization, and GDP per capita.

title: Weak States: Causes and Consequences of the Sicilian Mafia
context: The paper studies late-19th to 20th-century Sicily, focusing on municipalities (n≈333, sample ≈245) around the severe spring drought of 1893. In a weak-state environment, the drought amplified peasant mobilization via the Peasant Fasci in 1893–94, prompting landowners and local elites to enlist the Mafia; Mafia presence is measured in 1900 and outcomes (literacy, public goods, political competition) are followed in the 1910s–30s and into the 1960s–80s. Historical cadastral, rainfall, election, and vital-registration data provide the setting for the causal analysis.

title: Using Disasters to Estimate the Impact of Uncertainty
context: This paper analyzes quarterly country-level data for 59 countries from 1970Q1 to 2020Q1, using country-quarter observations of GDP growth, national stock-market returns and volatility. The authors exploit exogenous shocks — natural disasters, terrorist attacks, coups and revolutions — weighted by short-run jumps in U.S. newspaper coverage to instrument first- and second-moment (level and uncertainty) shocks in asset returns. The setting spans advanced and emerging economies, linking financial-market moments to short-run macro outcomes while accounting for spatial spillovers, alternative uncertainty proxies, and model-based simulations.

title: Government Old-Age Support and Labor Supply: Evidence from the Old Age Assistance Program

title: Wealth of Two Nations: The U.S. Racial Wealth Gap, 1860-2020
context: This paper constructs a continuous national series of white-to-Black per-capita wealth ratios in the United States from 1860 to 2020, using historical complete-count censuses, southern state tax records, Monroe Work’s Negro Year Book, and the Survey of Consumer Finances. The analysis situates wealth dynamics in institutional contexts—slavery, Emancipation, Reconstruction, Jim Crow, New Deal, the Civil Rights era, and post-1980 asset-price booms—and examines how initial conditions, income growth, savings behavior, and capital gains shaped long-run convergence and divergence.

title: The Development Effects of the Extractive Colonial Economy: The Dutch Cultivation System in Java
context: This paper examines the long-run local development effects of the Dutch 19th-century Cultivation System in Java (primarily the 1830s–1870s), centered on 94 colonial sugar factories and their contiguous catchment villages that were forced to grow cane. Using archival village-level records linked to modern Indonesian censuses and household surveys (1980–2011) and cohort data (born 1920s onward), the authors study impacts on villages, households, and local industry, focusing on infrastructure, industrial composition, land tenure, schooling, and consumption.

title: Liquidity Risk and Expected Stock Returns
context: This result is estimated using U.S. common stocks (NYSE, AMEX, NASDAQ) over 1966–1999. The authors construct a monthly aggregate liquidity innovation from signed-volume return-reversal regressions, predict stock-level liquidity betas, and form annual decile portfolios by predicted liquidity beta. They relate portfolio excess returns to market, size (SMB), value (HML), and momentum factors and find the top-minus-bottom liquidity-beta decile spread yields a significant positive abnormal return (about 7–10% annual, depending on the factor specification).

It analyzes a randomized cistern construction program implemented from 2012 across 40 municipalities (425 neighborhood clusters, ~1,308 eligible households) and municipal‑level rainfall shocks (including the severe 2012 drought), using baseline (Oct–Dec 2011) and follow‑up household surveys (Nov–Dec 2012 and 2013) linked to 2012 electronic voting‑machine returns. The institutional setting features limited formal insurance/credit, common clientelist exchanges with local politicians, and compulsory municipal elections in Oct 2012.

title: Local Labor Markets and Welfare Spells: Do Demand Conditions Matter?
context: This paper analyzes how local labor market conditions affected AFDC welfare spells in California using the administrative Longitudinal Database (10% sample) of monthly records, January 1987–December 1992, at the AFDC case/family level (AFDC-FG and AFDC-UP). County- and zip-code–level labor market measures (unemployment, employment, employment/population, sector earnings from UI/202 data) and 1990 Census neighborhood controls are used. For this triplet, the reported effect on the AFDC caseload is a simulation derived from the estimated discrete-time duration (logit hazard) model with county fixed effects, summarizing the implied permanent caseload change under the assumption of constant entry rates.

title: Is It Whom You Know or What You Know? An Empirical Assessment of the Lobbying Process
context: Using U.S. federal lobbying and campaign-disclosure records from 1999–2008, the paper studies individual lobbyists, lobbying reports, and Members of Congress (committee assignments) to assess whether lobbyists follow politicians or specialize by issue. Data combine Senate Office of Public Records lobbying registrations, FEC individual contribution records, committee/subcommittee assignments for the 106th–110th Congresses, and lobbyist biographies. Main causal tests exploit exogenous committee reassignments and within-pair changes over consecutive Congresses, with panel fixed-effects at lobbyist and report levels.

title: The Rise and Fall of World Trade, 1870-1939
context: This effect is estimated with gravity-style regressions on pooled bilateral trade (exports+imports) for the cross-sections 1913, 1928 and 1938 across a global sample (up to 56 countries). The unit of observation is country-pair trade; the institutional setting is the prewar and interwar era when adherence to the classical gold standard rose and then collapsed. Regressions report OLS and country fixed-effects estimates and use 2SLS (IV) as a robustness check for endogeneity of gold adherence.

unit_of_analysis: The unit of observation is country-pair trade

title: Bayesian Inference for Hospital Quality in a Selection Model
context: This empirical study evaluates hospital quality using 74,848 Medicare patients aged 65+ admitted for pneumonia to 114 Los Angeles County hospitals during 1989–1992. The outcome is ten-day in-hospital mortality from California discharge records; the setting is fee-for-service Medicare. The authors model nonrandom patient admission with a structural multinomial-probit choice model and exploit distances from patient ZIP centroids to alternative hospitals as exogenous variation, while controlling for demographics, disease stage, hospital size and ownership, estimating the model by Bayesian MCMC.

title: The Voice of Monetary Policy
context: This paper analyzes U.S. Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meetings and press conferences from 2011 through June 2019 (67 meetings, 36 press conferences), using 692 Chair Q&A answers by Ben Bernanke, Janet Yellen, and Jerome Powell. It quantifies vocal emotions via a custom deep‑learning speech classifier and textual sentiment, and links variation in voice tone to daily financial market responses (equity, volatility, bond and corporate ETFs, inflation expectations, and exchange rates), controlling for identified intraday policy shocks and the shadow federal funds rate.

title: Interfirm Relationships and Business Performance
context: A large-scale field experiment in Nanchang, China (2013–2015) studied owner-managed micro, small and medium enterprises established within the prior three years. From 2,800 interested firms researchers randomized 1,480 into treatment groups of ten managers who held monthly self-organized meetings for one year (Aug 2013–mid-2014) and 1,320 into a no-meetings control, with baseline (2013), midline (2014) and endline (2015) surveys. The program (run with the local CIIT) also randomized group composition, information shocks about financial products, and one-time cross-group meetings.

unit_of_analysis: owner-managed micro, small and medium enterprises

title: Wealth Taxation and Wealth Accumulation: Theory and Evidence from Denmark
context: This paper studies causal effects of Danish wealth-tax reforms—chiefly the 1989 cut from 2.2% to 1% and subsequent threshold changes leading up to abolition by 1997—using full-population administrative household wealth records (annual taxable wealth; causal analysis focuses on the 1980s–1990s, 1986–1996 event window). Units are individuals/households (emphasis on top percentiles, e.g., top 1% and 98–99th percentiles). Identification exploits institutional features: a horizontal tax ceiling, exemption thresholds (singles vs couples), third‑party reporting, and gradual reform timing.

title: General Equilibrium Effects of Cash Transfers: Experimental Evidence from Kenya
context: This paper analyzes the local general-equilibrium effects of large one-time cash transfers delivered by GiveDirectly to over 10,500 poor households across 653 villages (84 sublocations) in rural Siaya County, western Kenya. Transfers (≈USD 1,000 per eligible household, mid-2014 to early‑2017) amounted to roughly 15–24% of local GDP during rollout. The study uses household and enterprise censuses (≈65,000 households, 12,095 firms), representative household and enterprise surveys, and monthly market price data to trace impacts on consumption, assets, firm revenue, wages, and prices across households, firms, villages, and markets.

context: This paper uses firm- and plant-level panels (up to half a million firms; IT establishment sample ≈160,000) across 12 European countries over 1996–2007 to estimate how rising Chinese import competition — especially after China’s 2001 WTO accession and the phased abolition of textile/apparel quotas (MFA reforms in 2002 and 2005) — affected firm innovation (patents, R&D), IT adoption, TFP, employment and survival. title: Pricing Heart Attack Treatments
context: This paper empirically examines changes in the price and welfare of medical care for acute myocardial infarction (AMI) in the United States using detailed charge data from a major teaching hospital (1983–1994) and national Medicare claims (1984–1994). The unit of analysis is the individual AMI patient (with demographic adjustment focusing on elderly Medicare beneficiaries). The institutional setting includes evolving Medicare reimbursement, diffusion of procedures (catheterization, PTCA, CABG), and changing CPI billing practices; analyses decompose spending, construct SPIs and a COL, and evaluate survival improvements.

title: Multinational Firms:  Reconciling Theory and Evidence
context: This paper empirically examines how parent- and host-country characteristics shape foreign affiliate production (local sales versus export sales) using annual panel data for 1986–1994 on non-bank manufacturing affiliate sales between the United States and 36 other countries. The unit of observation is country-pair–year affiliate sales (inward and outward series). The analysis is embedded in a knowledge-capital multinational framework and uses covariates such as GDP and GDP differences, skilled-labor shares, trade and investment-cost indices, distance, and interaction terms to capture scale, endowment, and transaction-cost effects.
