title: The Economic Geography of Global Warming
context: The paper builds a global spatial dynamic assessment model quantified at 1°×1° grid cells using G-Econ, HDI, and other datasets to estimate how temperature changes (historical decades 1990–2005 for damage estimation, natality calibrated back to 1950–1999) affect local fundamental productivity, amenities, and natality. Units are grid-cell locations (population, wages, energy use) worldwide. The model combines empirical panel estimation of temperature damage functions with structural inversion, carbon-cycle calibration and climate downscaling to simulate migration, trade, innovation and policy counterfactuals from 2000 forward (projections to 2400).

title: Air Pollution and Infant Health: What Can We Learn From California's Recent Experience
context: This paper analyzes individual-level birth, fetal-death, and infant-mortality records in California over the 1990s (approximately 1989–2000), linking pregnancies and infants (by maternal 5-digit ZIP) to ambient pollution measures for four criteria pollutants (O3, CO, PM10, NO2) from monitoring stations. The institutional setting is California during a period of declining pollution and active air-quality regulation. The authors exploit within–ZIP-code temporal variation (controlling for ZIP fixed effects, month/year seasonality, weather) to estimate prenatal and postnatal pollution impacts on birth outcomes and infant mortality.

unit_of_analysis: individual-level birth, fetal-death, and infant-mortality records

title: The Effect of Female Education on Fertility and Infant Health: Evidence from School Entry Policies Using Exact Date of Birth
context: This study uses individual natality records from California (1989–2002) and Texas (1989–2001) on first-time mothers (focus on women ≤23) born in those states. It exploits state school-entry cutoffs (California: Dec 1; Texas: Sep 1) that create a discontinuity in schooling by exact date of birth to instrument for maternal education. The paper estimates effects of female education on fertility outcomes, prenatal behaviors, birthweight, prematurity, and infant mortality, leveraging local RD/IV methods and administrative birth and death records.

title: Performance Pay and Wage Inequality
context: This paper analyzes U.S. male workers (male household heads aged roughly 18–65) using the PSID panel primarily covering 1976–1998 (with key comparisons between late 1970s and early 1990s), supplemented by NLSY (late 1980s and late 1990s) and firm-survey evidence. It studies how the rising incidence of performance-pay (bonuses, commissions, piece-rates) across occupations and industries — against a backdrop of declining unionization and improved monitoring/IT — altered returns to observed and unobserved worker attributes and contributed to wage inequality.

unit_of_analysis: U.S. male workers (male household heads aged roughly 18–65)

title: Beauty and the Labor Market
context: Using individual-level survey data from three household studies—the 1971 Quality of American Life (U.S.), the 1977 Quality of Employment (U.S.), and the 1977/1979/1981 Canadian Quality of Life—the paper analyzes how interviewer-rated physical appearance relates to hourly earnings, labor-force participation and marriage outcomes. The unit of observation is working-age individuals (18–64) in the U.S. and Canada; analyses control for demographics, education, experience, occupation, health and interviewer effects, and use a three-year Canadian panel of appearance ratings to address measurement and sorting issues.

title: Long-Run Price Elasticities of Demand for Credit: Evidence from a Countrywide Field Experiment in Mexico
context: This paper reports a countrywide field experiment in Mexico beginning May 2007 with 29 months of follow-up, randomizing permanent interest-rate cuts (20 percentage-point vs. 10pp reductions from roughly 100% APR) across 80 geographic regions covering Compartamos Banco branches. Units include region-month aggregates and individual borrowers/groups using the Crédito Mujer group microloan product (female borrowers). The institutional setting is Mexico’s largest for‑profit microlender with group liability loans, weekly repayments, and credit‑bureau reporting; analyses combine administrative loan data, credit bureau snapshots, and competitor rate surveys.

title: Can Network Theory-based Targeting Increase Technology Adoption?
treatment: Being connected to two trained seeds
context: Randomized field experiment conducted in 200 rural villages in Malawi (Machinga, Mwanza, Nkhotakota) during 2011–2013. The unit of randomization is the village (two seed farmers trained per village) and household- and farmer-level outcomes were tracked (~5,600 households, ~30 per village). The study worked through the Malawi Ministry of Agriculture’s extension system—which relies on scarce extension agents and lead farmers—to test network-theory-based seed selection versus standard extension-selected seeds while promoting the novel pit-planting and crop-residue management technologies.

title: Prep School for Poor Kids: The Long-Run Impacts of Head Start on Human Capital and Economic Self-Sufficiency
context: This study estimates long-run effects of Head Start’s county-level rollout in the United States (1965–1980) on cohorts born 1950–1980, using linked 2000 Census and 2001–2018 ACS data merged to the SSA Numident. The unit is individuals (adults observed at ages 25–54), and identification exploits county Head Start launch dates combined with school-entry age eligibility (ages 3–5 vs age 6+), controlling for county fixed effects and state-by-birth-year trends. The setting features a rapid, idiosyncratic OEO grant roll-out and phased program implementation.

unit_of_analysis: unit is individuals (adults observed at ages 25–54)

title: What do Aggregate Consumption Euler Equations Say about the Capital Income Tax Burden?
context: This paper uses U.S. aggregate annual data (primarily 1947–1997) to study how capital income taxation affects the wedge between expected pre-tax returns on the capital stock and consumption growth. Units are national aggregates—BEA measures of pre-tax and after-tax capital returns, per-capita nondurable and services consumption, and capital-income tax revenue (federal, state, local). The analysis relies on consumption Euler equations, VARs to form expectations, and TSLS estimates to infer capital supply elasticity and tax incidence in the mid-to-late 20th century United States.

unit_of_analysis: Units are national aggregates—BEA measures of pre-tax and after-tax capital returns, per-capita nondurable and services consumption, and capital-income tax revenue (federal, state, local).

title: Fiscal Stimulus in a Monetary Union: Evidence from U.S. Regions
context: This paper studies the effects of regional government spending in a monetary union using U.S. state- and Census-division-level data on military procurement (1966–2006). Units are states and ten contiguous regions; outcomes include per-capita GDP, employment, and prices. title: Fiscal Policy, Profits, and Investment
context: This paper analyzes annual country-year panel data for 18 OECD countries (maximum span 1960–1996) using business-sector aggregates (investment, profits, sales, capital) and cyclically-adjusted fiscal variables (primary spending, public wages, transfers, and tax components). It studies how changes in fiscal policy and episodes of large fiscal adjustments affect profits and private business investment, emphasizing labor-market channels (public employment/wages and labor taxes) and the composition of spending versus taxation in advanced-economy macro settings.

Units are children (and mothers with sister comparisons); the sample is national with oversamples of African‑Americans and Hispanics. context: This paper studies the effects of the 1970 and 1977 U.S. Clean Air Act Amendments on manufacturing using plant-level Census of Manufactures data from 1967–1987. title: The Unequal Effects of Liberalization: Evidence from Dismantling the License Raj in India
context: This paper studies the effects of dismantling India’s “license raj” and concurrent trade liberalization on registered manufacturing across 16 Indian states over 1980–1997. Using state × three-digit industry-year observations from the Annual Survey of Industries, it exploits staggered nationwide delicensing waves (major waves in 1985 and 1991) and cross-state variation in labor-market institutions (state amendments to the 1947 Industrial Disputes Act) and tariffs to estimate how liberalization differentially affected output, employment, entry and investment.

title: Do You Know That I Know That You Know...? Higher-Order Beliefs in Survey Data
context: This paper uses two survey waves of New Zealand firms (initial: 2017Q4–2018Q1, follow‑up: 2018Q1–2018Q2) with a fresh sample of 1,025 firm managers (≥6 employees) and 515 reinterviews three months later. Respondents across manufacturing, professional/financial services and other sectors received randomized information treatments (average first‑order beliefs, average higher‑order beliefs, both, or past inflation). The study measures causal effects of these RCT treatments on managers’ first‑ and higher‑order inflation expectations and, via IV and panel regressions, on firm decisions (employment, investment, prices, wages).

title: The Use of Full-line Forcing Contracts in the Video Rental Industry
context: This study analyzes the U.S. video-rental market (1998–2002) using Rentrak transaction data on ~6,400 retail stores and 961 titles, focusing for this triplet on six large distributors over a 12-month window (months 30–41 of the panel). title: Disease and Development: The Effect of Life Expectancy on Economic Growth
context: This cross-country study analyzes country-level effects of the mid-20th-century “international epidemiological transition” (primarily 1940–1980) across about 59 countries (decadal country-year observations). The unit is the country; outcomes include life expectancy, population, total births, GDP and GDP per capita. Identification exploits exogenous timing of global health interventions (e.g., antibiotics, DDT, WHO campaigns) interacted with pre-1940 disease-specific mortality rates. The core institutional background is the global diffusion of medical and public-health technologies after WWII and resulting differential mortality declines by baseline disease burden.

title: Which Countries Have State Religions?
context: This paper analyzes determinants of official state religions for 188 countries using historical indicators of state-religion status in 1900 and observed outcomes in 1970 and 2000. The unit is the country; analyses account for major regime changes (independence, territorial splits, adoption/ending of Communism), measures of religious adherence concentration and faith shares, colonial and legal origins, and macro controls (per-capita GDP, region). title: The Q-Theory of Mergers
context: The paper develops a Q-theory of mergers and tests it on U.S. exchange-listed firms using Compustat and CRSP. Firm-level panel regressions cover 1971–2000 (investment, acquisitions, market-to-book Q, cash), while historical/time-series evidence on merger waves extends roughly 1890–1998. The institutional setting is the U.S. corporate investment and M&A market, modeling acquisitions as purchases of bundled used capital with fixed M&A costs and using market-to-book measures (Q and q) to explain firm- and aggregate-level reallocation.

title: Estimation of a Dynamic Auction Game
context: This paper estimates a dynamic first-price repeated procurement auction with capacity constraints using California Department of Transportation highway paving auction data from May 1996 to May 1999. Units are bidders (classified as regular and fringe) and contract-level sealed bids across 2,207 awarded projects. Institutional features include reserve prices, sealed-bid awarding to the low bidder, multi-month contract durations creating backlog and capacity effects, and observable contract characteristics. The authors use a structural two-stage estimator and reduced-form regressions to quantify backlog effects and efficiency losses.

title: The Welfare Effects of Social Media
context: This paper reports a randomized evaluation (and associated IV analysis) of deactivating Facebook among US users in the four weeks surrounding the November 2018 US midterm elections. The unit is individual Facebook users recruited via Facebook ads, who were paid (BDM) to deactivate for four weeks (Oct–Nov 2018); researchers verified deactivation via public profile checks and measured outcomes via surveys, daily texts, administrative voting records, and platform activity, focusing on time use, news knowledge, polarization, subjective well‑being, and post‑treatment demand.

title: Tracing the Impact of Bank Liquidity Shocks: Evidence from an Emerging Market
context: This paper studies how bank liquidity shocks following the unanticipated May 1998 India–Pakistan nuclear tests affected corporate lending and firm distress in Pakistan. Using the State Bank of Pakistan loan-level panel (July 1996–March 2000) covering ~22,000 loans to ~18,600 firms and 42 commercial banks, the authors exploit cross-bank variation in exposure to dollar deposit accounts and pre/post comparisons to isolate bank lending and firm borrowing channels. title: Imported Intermediate Inputs and Domestic Product Growth: Evidence from India

title: Unraveling Reduces the Scope of an Entry Level Labor Market: Gastroenterology With and Without a Centralized Match
context: This paper examines U.S. gastroenterology fellowship matches using physician-level AMA data for fellows finishing mostly between the late 1970s and 2003. It exploits the introduction of a centralized clearinghouse (MSMP/NRMP) that organized fellow appointments from 1986 (positions starting 1987) through the mid-1990s and its subsequent breakdown beginning ~1997–2000, alongside a 1996 policy shock that reduced positions and extended training, to compare individual mobility (hospital, city, state) across market regimes.

title: The Deposits Channel of Monetary Policy

The setting is U.S. local deposit markets (counties) and bank branches; units include branches, banks, and bank–county lending. The analysis leverages FOMC rate changes (weekly and quarterly), Fed-futures expected/unexpected decompositions, and geographic variation in local deposit-market concentration (HHI) under standard banking institutional assumptions.

title: Adverse Selection and an Individual Mandate: When Theory Meets Practice
context: This paper studies the 2006 Massachusetts health reform — notably the individual mandate, Commonwealth Choice exchange, and existing community-rating and guaranteed-issue rules — and its effect on the individual health insurance market. title: Employment Structure and the Rise of the Modern Tax System

The main causal test exploits variation in the timing of state Industrial Development Bonds (IDB) implementation—specifically the lag between legislative vote-in and state supreme-court upholding—to estimate effects on individual employment structure, the personal income tax exemption threshold, and state income tax revenue. Units are individuals/households for distributional profiles and U.S. states for the policy-event analysis; key institutions include revenue bonds and IRS information-exchange agreements.

title: Information Technology, Workplace Organization and the Demand for Skilled Labor:  Firm-Level Evidence
context: This paper examines how information technology, workplace organization, and human capital jointly affect firm-level labor demand and productivity in the United States. The analysis focuses on firms as the unit of observation and situates results in the context of falling IT prices and IT-enabled organizational change driving skill-biased technical change.

title: Organized Crime and Economic Growth: Evidence from Municipalities Infiltrated by the Mafia
context: This empirical study evaluates long-run effects of city-council dismissals (CCDs) for alleged Mafia infiltration in Italy, analyzing 245 CCD events from 1991–2016 across municipalities in ten regions. It uses administrative employer–employee social security data (1983–2017), real-estate, public-procurement, politician, and municipal finance records to measure impacts on firms, employment, wages, and real-estate. title: Consumer Spending During Unemployment: Positive and Normative Implications
context: Using de-identified JPMorgan Chase monthly bank-account data from January 2014–June 2016 across 20 U.S. states, the paper studies roughly 182,000 households who received unemployment insurance (UI) by direct deposit. It exploits variation in UI generosity, timing, and duration (most states six months; e.g., Florida four months, New Jersey unusually generous and no waiting period) to trace high-frequency household income, nondurable spending categories, and job‑finding hazards around UI receipt and predictable benefit exhaustion.

title: Evidence on Growth, Increasing Returns and the Extent of the Market
context: This triplet is estimated on the cross‑country sample of 65 low‑income countries (those with 1960 per‑capita GDP roughly ≤ $1,500), using data for 1960–1985. Units are countries; openness is measured by the share of total trade (exports+imports of goods and non‑factor services) in GDP, and the outcome is average per‑capita GDP growth over 1960–85. The paper implements unconditional cross‑sectional growth regressions for this sample and explicitly notes the openness measure may be endogenous.

title: Performance Pay and Productivity
context: This paper analyzes a 19-month panel (Jan 1994–July 1995) of installers at Safelite Glass Corporation (Columbus, Ohio), using monthly person-month observations (≈31,000 good observations on ~3,700 installers) to study effects of a phased shift from hourly wages to piece-rate pay with a wage guarantee (≈$11/hr, ≈$20 per unit) instituted after a management change. The setting is commercial auto‑glass installation, with firm administrative data on units per associate per day, pay, hours, and separations; implementation was staggered across workers allowing within-worker before–after comparisons.

title: On the Size Distribution of Macroeconomic Disasters
context: This paper analyzes long-run national-account annual per-capita consumption and GDP for up to 36 countries (data back to as early as 1870 through 2006) to identify macroeconomic “disasters” (peak-to-trough contractions ≥10%) and characterize their size distribution. title: Acquisitions, Productivity, and Profitability: Evidence from the Japanese Cotton Spinning Industry
context: This paper studies plant- and firm-level effects of acquisitions in the Japanese cotton-spinning industry between 1896–1920 (acquisitions 1898–1920). title: Monetary Policy Shifts and the Term Structure
context: This paper analyzes U.S. quarterly macro and bond market data from June 1952–December 2007, using three-month T-bill rates, zero-coupon Treasury yields, the output gap, and inflation. title: Factor Market Failures and the Adoption of Irrigation in Rwanda
context: This paper evaluates government-built hillside irrigation schemes in Karongi and Nyanza districts of Rwanda (construction 2009–2014) using plot- and household-level panel surveys (baseline 2015 with follow-ups through 2018). The unit of analysis is cultivator households and their plots near a gravity-fed canal ‘command area’ on terraced hillsides. Key institutional details: gravity-fed valves, terracing, formal land titles, seasonal rainy/dry cycles, and schemes initially operated with no routine water fees. The design exploits the command-area boundary to identify causal effects.

title: Building State Capacity: Evidence from Biometric Smartcards in India
context: This paper evaluates a large-scale randomized rollout (2010–2012) of biometric Smartcards for benefit disbursement in rural Andhra Pradesh, India. The government randomized the order of conversion of mandals/sub-districts (covering ~19 million people across eight districts) and linked Smartcards to bank accounts to pay NREGS workfare participants and SSP pension recipients. Using baseline (2010) and endline (2012) household and administrative data, the study measures impacts on payment timeliness, leakage, beneficiary time costs, and program access within the state-run welfare delivery system.

title: High Wage Workers and High Wage Firms
context: This paper uses linked employer–employee panels for France to decompose total annual compensation into worker- and firm-level components. It follows roughly a 1/25 sample of private-sector workers (about 1 million observations, 1976–1987) and a probability sample of firms (≈20,000 firms followed 1978–1988, with firm-level data linked via SIREN), measuring real annual compensation (wages plus benefits). The setting is French private employment and social‑security reporting; analyses exploit within-person mobility and firm averages to relate high‑wage workers and high‑wage firms to productivity, capital intensity and survival.

title: Uncertainty Shocks, Asset Supply and Pricing over the Business Cycle
context: This paper analyzes the US nonfinancial corporate sector using quarterly postwar U.S. data (1959Q1–2011Q3) to estimate a business-cycle DSGE model with endogenous firm financing and ambiguity-averse households. Firms’ investment, leverage and shareholder payout decisions are modeled and matched to aggregate series; shocks include marginal product of capital and a fixed operating cost, with Markov-switching volatility and time-varying ambiguity. Estimation uses likelihood-based Bayesian methods on an MS-DSGE representation to infer causal effects of uncertainty shocks on asset prices and financial flows.

title: The Impact of Public School Choice: Evidence from Los Angeles' Zones of Choice
context: This paper studies Los Angeles Unified School District’s Zones of Choice (ZOC) reform—implemented starting around 2012–2013—using administrative data from roughly 2008–2019. It evaluates effects on high-school students (chiefly 11th graders and graduating cohorts) and market-level school outcomes across ZOC versus non-ZOC neighborhoods in Los Angeles. ZOC combined catchment areas, expanded within-district choice with centralized rank-ordered (immediate-acceptance) assignment and lotteries for oversubscription, thereby changing competitive incentives among attendance-zone schools.

title: Why the Apple Doesn't Fall Far: Understanding Intergenerational Transmission of Human Capital
context: This estimate uses Norwegian administrative and census data exploiting the staggered 1960–1972 municipal implementation of a reform raising compulsory schooling from 7 to 9 years as an instrument for parental education. It focuses on parents born 1947–1958 (linked to their municipality of upbringing) and their adult children (aged 20–35 in 2000); the causal IV (2SLS) estimates for fathers are primarily identified using the reform's effects on parents in the lower tail (≤9 years) of the education distribution.

title: A Rehabilitation of Monetary Policy in the 1950s
context: This paper examines U.S. monetary policy in the 1950s — primarily 1952–1958 after the 1951 Treasury–Fed Accord — and contrasts it with later eras (1964–1979, 1979–1987, 1987–2000). Using Federal Reserve FOMC minutes and quarterly macro data (federal funds rate, GDP deflator, real GDP), it assesses policymakers’ motives and actions and estimates a forward-looking policy rule to evaluate how the Fed adjusted nominal rates to expected inflation and output gaps.

unit_of_analysis: quarterly macro data (federal funds rate, GDP deflator, real GDP)

title: Credit Market Consequences of Improved Personal Identification: Field Experimental Evidence from Malawi
context: This randomized field experiment (baseline Sept 2007; treatment and loan season 2007–08; follow-up Aug–Nov 2008) was conducted in rural Malawi (Dedza, Mchinji, Dowa, Kasungu) among smallholder farmers organized in clubs applying for in-kind paprika input loans (3,206 applicants; 1,147 borrowers). Partner institutions were the Malawi Rural Finance Corporation (lender) and Cheetah Paprika (buyer). In this setting with weak formal identification and limited formal credit, fingerprinting of applicants aimed to strengthen identification and the credibility of lenders’ dynamic (future-credit) incentives; the reported effect on loan size is conditional on borrowers who took loans.

context: Using US firm-level data (CRSP–Compustat matched firms combined with SPECTRUM institutional-holdings data), the paper analyzes 1983–1998 (23,501 firm-years) to study how stock prices and institutional vs. individual ownership respond to firm cash-flow news. title: Do Foreign Investors Destabilize Stock Markets?  The Korean Experience  in 1997
context: This paper examines how foreign investors traded and affected stock prices on the Korean Stock Exchange using transaction-level trades and daily foreign-ownership data from Nov 30, 1996 through Dec 1997 (covering the Oct–Dec 1997 Korean crisis). title: Trust and Delegation

title: How is Macro News Transmitted to Exchange Rates?

title: The Economics of “Buy Now, Pay Later”: A Merchant’s Perspective
context: This paper studies the merchant economics of Buy-Now-Pay-Later (BNPL) using microdata from a German e-commerce furniture retailer. It combines an early-2022 randomized controlled trial that randomized display of a pay-in-30 BNPL option at checkout (~75k customers) with 2021 observational data on ~595k BNPL applications (customer credit scores, cart margins, approvals, defaults) and county-level panels (2016–2019) of checkouts to assess default cyclicality. Units include customers, shopping carts/transactions, BNPL applications, and counties; BNPL is offered in-house and subsidized by the merchant.

unit_of_analysis: Units include customers, shopping carts/transactions, BNPL applications, and counties

title: Does the Failure of the Expectations Hypothesis Matter for Long-Term Investors
treatment: Failing to hedge time-varying risk premia
context: The paper studies U.S. nominal bond and equity markets from 1952–1998 using monthly zero-coupon yields (3-month to 10-year), CPI inflation, and broad stock-index returns. It estimates a three-factor affine term-structure model with time-varying risk premia and expected inflation to analyze portfolio choice of a long-horizon investor allocating between nominal bonds, a stock portfolio, and the nominal riskfree asset. The institutional setting is the U.S. Treasury market with nominal bonds (possible inflation incompleteness) and investors facing predictable term premia and inflation risk.

title: Are Perks Purely Managerial Excess?
context: Using a panel of more than 300 large U.S. publicly traded firms (primarily Fortune 500) over 1986–1999, the paper studies CEO and divisional-manager perquisite (perk) offerings. Unit of observation is firm-year and position (CEO or divisional manager), with rich Hewitt Associates compensation-survey data supplemented by Compustat, airport flight counts, county population/commute times, and state tax data. The setting is U.S. corporate compensation practice, examining whether perks reflect managerial private benefits or productivity, controlling for industry and year effects and limited within-firm time variation.

title: Evaporating Liquidity
context: This paper studies US equity markets from January 1998 to December 2010, using daily CRSP data on NYSE, AMEX and Nasdaq stocks and 48 Fama–French industries. It analyzes returns of short‑term reversal trading strategies as proxies for liquidity provision by market makers, algorithmic traders, and hedge funds, focusing on market‑turmoil episodes including LTCM (1998), the 2000–01 Nasdaq collapse, and the 2007–09 financial crisis, and accounting for institutional changes such as Nasdaq order‑handling reforms and decimalization.

title: Learning about CEO Ability and Stock Return Volatility
context: This paper examines market learning about CEO ability using 1,873 CEO turnovers at 1,582 U.S. publicly traded firms between 1992 and 2006 (volatility data through 2009). The unit of analysis is firm-months and firm-CEO pairs, using monthly realized, idiosyncratic, and option-implied volatilities. A theoretical Bayesian learning model (dividend/earnings GBM, efficient markets) motivates tests; empirical analyses compare exogenous (death/retirement) vs forced turnovers and control for post-turnover actions, firm characteristics, and calendar effects.

title: How Costly is Financial (not Economic) Distress? Evidence from Highly Leveraged Transactions that Became Distressed
treatment: Fraction of debt owed to banks
context: Analyzes U.S. highly leveraged transactions (HLTs)—primarily management buyouts and leveraged recapitalizations completed in the 1980s (mostly after 1985)—following 31 firms from pre-transaction through financial distress resolution (through Dec 1995). Unit of observation is the firm; data come from SEC filings, CRSP, COMPUSTAT and Value Line industry benchmarks. The institutional setting emphasizes high leverage, junk bonds versus bank debt, and Chapter 11 restructurings, aiming to isolate costs of financial (not economic) distress by comparing firm outcomes to market and industry performance.

title: Managing Bank Liquidity Risk: How Deposit-Loan Synergies Vary with Market Conditions
context: This paper empirically analyzes large U.S. banks from 1990–2002, using weekly stock-return volatility (GARCH/realized) for top publicly traded domestic banks (about 170 firms, ~50,000 bank-week observations) matched to prior-quarter call-report balances (unused loan commitments, transactions deposits, liquidity and capital ratios). It leverages panel variation, cross-sectional comparisons, and market-wide episodes of tight short-term funding—proxied by the commercial paper–T-bill spread (notably the 1998 liquidity shock)—within the U.S. institutional setting of deposit insurance and central-bank backstops.

region_bucket: within the U.S. institutional setting of deposit insurance and central-bank backstops

title: Launching with a Parachute: The Gig Economy and New Business Formation
context: This empirical paper analyzes quarterly data from 2001–2016 for US incorporated places (population≥10,000), exploiting the staggered rollout of ridehailing services (UberX/Lyft) across cities as a proxy for the arrival of platform-enabled gig-economy opportunities. Units are cities/places (matched to DMAs/counties) and outcomes include new business registrations, SBA 7(a) loans to newly registered firms, and Google search interest. Identification relies on variation in treatment timing, city and quarter-year fixed effects, city-specific trends, and event-time dynamics; wage-growth volatility and credit proxies are used for heterogeneity tests.

title: The Corporate Governance Role of the Media: Evidence from Russia
context: The paper analyzes 98 alleged corporate-governance violation events at Russian firms drawn from Troika Dialog’s Bulletin over late 1998–2002, focusing on whether Anglo-American press coverage (FT, WSJ) and Hermitage Fund activism affected reversal of these decisions within about a year. The setting is Russia’s weak legal enforcement and low domestic media credibility; units are firm-level governance events. Hermitage’s December 1998 portfolio composition is used as exogenous variation in press lobbying.

region_bucket: The paper analyzes 98 alleged corporate-governance violation events at Russian firms

title: Managerial Ownership Dynamics and Firm Value
outcome: Probability of large decrease in managerial ownership
context: This study examines U.S. publicly traded single-class firms from 1988–2003 (approximately 4,925 firms and 22,000 firm-year observations), using Compact Disclosure, CRSP, and Compustat data on directors' and officers' equity stakes. It analyzes economically significant insider ownership changes (managerial purchases, sales, and changes in shares outstanding) and their relation to Tobin’s q and stock returns, excluding financials and utilities, and accounting for firm heterogeneity via panel methods with firm and year fixed effects.

title: Corporate Flexibility in a Time of Crisis
context: This paper studies how financial, workplace (ability to work remotely), and investment flexibility shaped U.S. firms' hiring and capital‑spending decisions during the COVID‑19 shock. It analyzes firm‑level CFO survey responses collected March–December 2020 (and a March 2019 survey for investment flexibility) covering public and private firms across industries and 47 states, and validates findings with Compustat and BLS industry data. The authors contrast COVID‑era effects with the 2008 financial crisis to isolate pandemic‑specific workplace impacts.

title: Mark-Up Pricing in Mergers and Acquisitions
context: This empirical study analyzes 1,398 successful takeovers of NYSE- and Amex-listed target firms in the United States during 1975–1991 (main sample 1,173 deals consummated within a year). Using CRSP returns, Dow Jones/Wall Street Journal deal announcements and Comment’s M&A database, it studies pre-bid stock price runups, post-announcement markups, trading volume, and the role of insider trading and legal changes (Insider Trading Sanctions Act 1984, ITSFEA 1988) across deal types (tender offers, auctions, MBOs, cash/equity payments).

title: R-Squared Around the World: New Theory and New Tests
context: This triplet examines, across 43 countries (1990–2001), how a country-level DISCLOSURE score — constructed by averaging responses in the Global Competitiveness Report surveys (1999–2000) on financial disclosure and availability of information (thus effectively time-invariant in the sample) — predicts country-year average stock-market synchronicity (average firm R2). Regressions (Table 4.1) use country-year R2 as the outcome and control for kurtosis, local market volatility and the MYY control set.

title: Noise in Expectations: Evidence from Analyst Forecasts
context: This paper studies noise and bias in equity analysts’ fiscal-year EPS forecasts for US publicly traded firms using I/B/E/S, Compustat, and CRSP data covering earnings announcements from 1990–2020. The unit of analysis is firm-year consensus forecasts at 1–3 year horizons formed within 45 days of annual earnings. The institutional setting is the US equity analyst market; authors compare analyst forecasts to supervised machine-learning econometric forecasts (rolling five-year windows) and estimate a structural model of reliance on noisy cognitive defaults.

title: Naïve *Buying* Diversification and Narrow Framing by Individual Investors
context: Using transaction-level brokerage data from Barclays Wealth (April 2012–June 2016) in the UK, the paper studies individual investor buy-day behavior. The unit of analysis is investor accounts and buy-day transactions (single- and multiple-stock and ETF purchases); samples include new accounts with full portfolio histories. The institutional setting is a mainstream online brokerage platform (SEDOL-identified UK-listed securities) where investors enter monetary amounts per trade. The paper combines descriptive evidence, cross-sectional regressions and simulations to evaluate Naïve Buying Diversification versus portfolio balancing and its welfare implications.

title: When Does a Mutual Fund's Trade Reveal its Skill?
context: This paper studies US active equity mutual funds and the stocks they trade using quarterly holdings and tick-by-tick transactions from 1983–2004. Units are mutual fund-quarter observations and individual stocks (NYSE, AMEX, NASDAQ). The authors infer trades from quarter-end holdings, measure information-events with the probability of informed trading (PIN) and alternative measures, and evaluate fund performance using Carhart four-factor alphas and characteristics-based (DGTW) selectivity, controlling for PIN risk to examine how trading in high-PIN stocks reveals manager skill and persistence.

title: Fooling Some of the People All of the Time: The Inefficient Performance and Persistence of Commodity Trading Advisors
context: Using Lipper-TASS performance data for commodity trading advisors (CTAs) reporting in the U.S. markets, the paper studies CTA performance, fees, and persistence over 1994–2007. The unit of observation is individual CTA funds (and equally-weighted CTA indices); the institutional setting emphasizes voluntary reporting to vendor databases, resulting in backfill, survivorship and look-back biases, and a regulatory/background comparison to earlier publicly-offered commodity pools. Analyses adjust for fees (estimating gross returns) and benchmark CTA returns to public dynamic futures-based strategies.

title: Wealth Destruction on a Massive Scale? A Study of Acquiring-Firm Returns in the Recent Merger Wave
context: Analyzes U.S. corporate acquisition announcements from 1980–2001, focusing on the 1998–2001 merger wave. Unit of observation is acquiring firms' announcement returns (public, private, and subsidiary acquisitions) drawn from SDC/CRSP/Compustat. The setting is the late-1990s high-valuation equity market with increased equity-financed deals, takeover defenses, and managerial incentives (stock/options). The paper links announcement abnormal and dollar returns to firm valuation (Tobin’s q, BM), deal characteristics, and post-announcement firm performance to infer causal effects of acquisitions and informational signaling.

title: Employee Costs of Corporate Bankruptcy
context: This paper studies U.S. public-firm bankruptcies (primarily Chapter 11) using employer–employee LEHD data (earnings 1985–2008) merged to Compustat and the UCLA-LoPucki BRD, focusing on bankruptcy filings from roughly 1991–2005. The unit of analysis is individual workers matched to firms (about 140 bankrupt firms and ~277,000 affected workers), with attention to local labor-market thickness, firm size, and liquidation versus reorganization. Analyses control for industry×year and county×year shocks to isolate bankruptcy-related effects on worker earnings and implied wage premia for bankruptcy risk.

title: Behavioralize This!  International Evidence on Autocorrelation Patterns of Stock Index and Futures Returns
context: This paper analyzes daily returns on stock indices and their corresponding futures contracts across 24 contracts in 15 countries, using contract-specific samples spanning primarily 1982–1999 (ending Feb 1999). The unit of observation is daily index and futures returns (with volume, open interest, interest/dividend data). The empirical setting exploits spot–futures no-arbitrage and variation in trading volume and transaction-cost bounds to test whether short-horizon positive autocorrelations arise from market microstructure (nonsynchronous trading, stale/bid–ask prices) or behavioral partial-adjustment models.

title: Bad Credit, No Problem? Credit and Labor Market Consequences of Bad Credit Reports
context: This empirical study analyzes U.S. individual bankruptcy filers (aged ~23–47 at filing) who filed between 1995–2004, exploiting that credit bureaus voluntarily remove Chapter 13 bankruptcy flags after seven years but Chapter 7 flags after ten. title: Jumps and Stochastic Volatility: Exchange Rate Processes Implicit in thePHLX Deutschemark Options
context: Analyzes PHLX-traded Deutschemark option transactions and synchronous $/DM futures (Jan 1984–Jun 1991), using a weekly panel of option trades (morning Wednesdays; ~19,689 transactions) and noon futures quotes from the CME. title: Do ETFs Increase Volatility?
context: The paper studies U.S. equity markets from roughly 2000–2012, focusing on stocks (S&P 500 and Russell 3000 constituents) and 660 U.S. ‘‘plain vanilla’’ equity ETFs traded on U.S. exchanges. Using intraday TAQ data, monthly holdings, ETF AUM and NAVs, and stock lending fees, the authors examine how ETF ownership and arbitrage (creation/redemption and NAV mispricing) affect intraday and daily volatility and turnover. They exploit institutional details of ETF arbitrage and the annual Russell index reconstitution to identify causal effects.

title: International Asset Allocation with Time-Varying Correlations
context: For a U.S. CRRA investor using monthly MSCI total-return data (Jan 1970–Dec 1997) for the US, UK and Germany, the paper’s RS Beta (two-state Markov regime‑switching) models compare optimal portfolios with hedged versus unhedged foreign equity. Currency hedging benefits are measured by certainty-equivalent economic compensation: e.g., for a one‑year horizon with risk aversion y=5, failing to hedge costs about 70 basis points, and hedging delivers roughly half the total benefit of international diversification.

title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation of Stochastic Volatility Models
context: This paper develops closed-form maximum-likelihood estimation for continuous-time stochastic-volatility diffusions and applies it to US S&P 500 index option markets. Empirical analyses use daily and weekly S&P 500 index levels and the CBOE VIX (a 30-day at‑the‑money implied‑volatility proxy) from January 2, 1990 to September 30, 2003 to estimate models (Heston, GARCH, lognormal, CEV). Units are daily/weekly market observations; option pricing and implied‑volatility inversion map observed prices into latent volatility for structural parameter identification. title: The U.S. listing gap
context: This analysis focuses on U.S. exchange‑listed firms across the 49 Fama–French industries using CRSP data, comparing listings at the 1996 peak to counts through 2012 (post‑peak). It tests whether industry reallocation explains the aggregate decline in listed firms and finds listings fell in 48 of 49 industries (only Non‑Metallic and Industrial Metal Mining unchanged), thereby rejecting an industry‑composition explanation for the overall drop.

title: Patent Collateral, Investor Commitment, and the Market for Venture Lending
context: The paper studies U.S. venture‑backed startups (startup‑year observations) in software, semiconductor, and medical‑device sectors founded 1987–1999 and followed through 2008. It links USPTO patent‑assignment records to identify patent‑secured loans, constructs sector‑level patent‑market liquidity and firm‑specificity measures, and exploits the March 2000 technology‑bubble collapse and heterogeneity in VC fund vintages to assess how patent market trading and VC credible commitment affect the likelihood of venture lending.

title: Equity-Holding Institutional Lenders:  Do they Receive Better Terms?
context: This paper analyzes U.S. leveraged loan syndicates during 1997–2007, using loan-tranche level observations for non‑financial publicly listed firms. It focuses on cases where non‑bank institutional lenders held equity stakes (>0.1%) in borrowers prior to origination (“dual holders”), measuring all‑in‑drawn spreads over LIBOR, tranche-level pricing within multi‑tranche loans, lender characteristics (hedge funds, private equity, mutual funds, etc.), and post‑loan uses of proceeds to evaluate whether dual holders earn premiums in the syndicated leveraged‑loan market.

title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation of Latent Affine Processes
context: This paper develops a maximum-likelihood, characteristic-function–based filtration for latent affine processes and applies it to 11,076 daily S&P 500 returns (United States) from 1953–1996. Observations are daily index returns; the economic setting is continuous-time stochastic volatility and jump-diffusion models used for asset pricing and option valuation. The study compares parameter and filtration estimates to prior EMM/SNP results, assesses time-varying volatility and jump intensities (including volatility-dependent arrival rates), and validates methods via Monte Carlo simulation.

title: Intermediary Balance Sheets and the Treasury Yield Curve
context: This paper studies the U.S. Treasury market across the pre- and post-Global Financial Crisis era through the March 2020 COVID episode (roughly 2000s–2020). It analyzes maturity-specific Treasury yields, OIS swap rates, cross-currency basis (CIP deviations), and primary dealers’ net Treasury positions (FR2004) as market-level time series and maturity buckets. The institutional setting includes post-GFC regulatory shifts (higher balance-sheet costs, SLR), large increases in Treasury issuance, and Fed policy interventions (asset purchases, swap lines). Dealers are treated as balance-sheet-constrained arbitrageurs.

title: Do ETFs Increase Volatility?
context: The paper studies U.S. equity markets from roughly 2000–2012, focusing on stocks (S&P 500 and Russell 3000 constituents) and 660 U.S. ‘‘plain vanilla’’ equity ETFs traded on U.S. exchanges. Using intraday TAQ data, monthly holdings, ETF AUM and NAVs, and stock lending fees, the authors examine how ETF ownership and arbitrage (creation/redemption and NAV mispricing) affect intraday and daily volatility and turnover. They exploit institutional details of ETF arbitrage and the annual Russell index reconstitution to identify causal effects.

title: The Geography of Financial Misconduct
context: This paper analyzes firm-level financial misconduct in the United States from roughly 1970–2010 across twenty large Economic Areas (metropolitan regions). title: Are Corporate Default Probabilities Consistent with the Static Tradeoff Theory?
context: This empirical study analyzes US nonfinancial firms (firm-year observations) from 1985–2008 (46,219 obs), testing whether firm default probabilities (S&P issuer ratings and Moody’s KMV EDF) reflect static tradeoff predictions. title: Arbitrage Asymmetry and the Idiosyncratic Volatility Puzzle
context: This paper studies U.S. equity markets from August 1965 to January 2011, using individual common stocks and value-weighted portfolios to examine how idiosyncratic volatility relates to average returns. The authors combine 11 documented return anomalies to construct a cross-sectional mispricing ranking, adjust returns with Fama–French three-factor regressions, and use the Baker–Wurgler investor-sentiment index (and macro controls) to study time-varying mispricing and arbitrage asymmetry driven by short-sale impediments and arbitrage risk.

title: Comovement
context: This paper studies comovement of US equities by analyzing S&P 500 inclusion and deletion events between 1976 and 2000 (inclusions Sept 22, 1976–Dec 31, 2000; deletions Jan 1, 1979–Dec 31, 2000). It exploits index redefinitions—additions/removals that change a stock's category/habitat but not its fundamentals—using daily, weekly, and monthly return windows, matching, and calendar-time tests to infer trading-induced effects on betas, R2, and cross-category correlations.

title: Do Analysts Herd? An Analysis of Recommendations and Market Reactions
context: This paper studies US sell-side analysts’ stock recommendation revisions and subsequent stock price reactions using IBES and CRSP data from November 1993–December 2005. Observations are analyst-stock-day recommendation revisions (upgrades/downgrades) with at least two other active analysts, focusing on one-level changes. The setting is US equity markets; the model assumes efficient prices incorporate public consensus and revisions. Cross-sectional variation includes analyst experience, brokerage size, and pre-revision dispersion; event windows around revision dates (and earnings) are used to infer market interpretation of herding incentives.

title: Capital-Market Effects of Securities Regulation: Prior Conditions, Implementation, and Enforcement
context: The paper studies firm-level stock-market liquidity across 26 European countries from 2001–2011 (about 112,260 firm-quarter observations, ~4,846 firms), exploiting staggered national transposition dates of two EU directives—the Market Abuse Directive (MAD) and the Transparency Directive (TPD)—that tightened insider-trading and disclosure rules. It analyzes regulated versus unregulated market listings, constructs a liquidity factor from bid-ask spreads, price impact, zero returns, and trading costs, and examines heterogeneity by prior regulatory quality, supervisory powers, penalties, and regulator staffing while controlling for other FSAP directives.

title: Labor-Force Heterogeneity and Asset Prices: the Importance of Skilled Labor
context: This paper examines how industry-level labor-skill heterogeneity shapes firm risk and asset prices using U.S. publicly traded firms (mainly 1991–2012, with a longer 1963–2012 extension via imputed skills) and G7 international data (1992–2012). Units are firm-year observations and annually rebalanced July–June portfolios. Industries are classified by the fraction of high-skilled occupations (DOT SVP and BLS OES). Empirical tests relate firms’ hiring rates to future stock returns under a production-investment model that assumes higher labor adjustment costs for high-skilled workers.

title: Capital-Market Effects of Securities Regulation: Prior Conditions, Implementation, and Enforcement
context: The paper studies firm-level stock-market liquidity across 26 European countries from 2001–2011 (about 112,260 firm-quarter observations, ~4,846 firms), exploiting staggered national transposition dates of two EU directives—the Market Abuse Directive (MAD) and the Transparency Directive (TPD)—that tightened insider-trading and disclosure rules. title: Fraudulent Income Overstatement on Mortgage Applications during the Credit Expansion of 2002 to 2005
context: This paper analyzes ZIP-code-level mortgage income overstatement in the United States during the housing credit boom—focusing on 2002–2005 but using data from 1991–2007 for comparisons. Using mortgage-application incomes (HMDA and lender data), IRS zip-level incomes, and fraud indicators (non‑agency/low-doc shares, InterThinx, Piskorski et al.), the authors study how income misreporting in low‑income, low‑credit‑score ZIP codes—especially in the non‑GSE securitization market—relates to mortgage origination growth, later defaults, and subsequent local income, poverty, and employment outcomes.

title: Do Bulls and Bears Move Across Borders? International Transmission of Stock Returns and Volatility as the World Turns
context: This triplet concerns intraday transmission from New York daytime S&P 500 returns to Tokyo overnight Nikkei 225 opening returns using intraday index data for September 1985–December 1989 (pre/around/post Oct 1987 Crash). Market-level daytime (open-to-close) and overnight (close-to-open) returns are measured with 10:00am openings to mitigate stale-quote bias. The paper finds a generally positive contemporaneous impact of New York daytime returns on Tokyo overnight returns over the full sample, but reports that this contemporaneous effect was not significant after the October 1987 Crash (with a separate lagged NY→Tokyo spillover appearing post‑Crash).

title: Mark-Up Pricing in Mergers and Acquisitions

title: Efficiency and the Bear: Short Sales and Markets around the World

title: The Legal Origins of Financial Development: Evidence from the Shanghai Concessions
context: This paper studies how transplanting British common law versus French civil law affected financial development across Western concessions in China, focusing on Shanghai (1845–1936) and additional concessions in Tianjin, Hankou, and Guangzhou. Units are concession- and grid-cell-level observations and firms (banks, qianzhuang, stock companies, and stock returns). Key institutional shocks are the 1869 creation of Mixed Courts (strengthening British/French legal jurisdiction) and the 1926–27 rendition returning courts to Chinese authority; authors exploit these temporal and spatial discontinuities to identify causal effects.

context: The paper analyzes U.S. homeowners and residential properties primarily over 2001–2013 (permit data 1994–2013) using monthly household panel data from the Consumer Expenditure Survey and property-level BuildFax permit records for ~9 million homes. Using country‑level time series — equity returns, dividend yields, market‑cap/GDP, trading turnover, net U.S. holdings and credit ratings — the authors date endogenous structural breaks in VARs and examine pre/post changes. The institutional setting comprises staggered policy reforms (ADRs, country funds, foreign‑ownership caps, exchange‑rate and regulatory reforms) associated with opening domestic equity markets to international investors.

title: Locked in by Leverage: Job Search during the Housing Crisis
context: This paper studies how housing-market distress during the Great Recession (home values peaked in Jan 2006; sample period May 2008–Dec 2009) affected job-seekers’ applications across the U.S. Using nearly 4 million applications to financial‑services jobs on a large online platform from applicants in 12,157 ZIP codes and all fifty states, the authors aggregate to ZIP‑code–months and exploit variation in ZIP‑level house price declines, mortgage leverage, and state mortgage recourse laws to assess changes in the geographic breadth and types of job search.

unit_of_analysis: nearly 4 million applications to financial-services jobs from applicants

title: Capital Structure and Debt Structure
context: This paper studies the capital and debt structure of U.S. publicly traded non‑financial firms using detailed balance‑sheet debt and origination data from SEC 10‑K filings and databases for 1996–2006. Units are firm‑year observations (random sample of 305 firms) and a panel of 140 “fallen angels” downgraded by Moody’s; the fallen‑angel analysis traces firms two years before through two years after downgrade events. The institutional setting emphasizes issuer credit ratings, debt priority, covenants, and access to bank versus arm’s‑length finance.

title: Cronyism and Capital Controls: Evidence from Malaysia
context: Analyzes how the 1997–98 Asian financial crisis and Malaysia’s September 1998 capital controls affected market valuations of 424 Malaysian listed firms. Using Gomez and Jomo’s coding of political ties (Mahathir- and Anwar-connected firms), the paper examines July 1997–August 1998 (crisis) and the September 1998 policy/political shock (capital controls and Anwar’s dismissal), exploiting variation in political connections, Bumiputera status, and prior foreign listings to infer changes in expected subsidies and access to international finance.

title: As Certain as Debt and Taxes: Estimating the Tax Sensitivity of Leverage from Exogenous State Tax Changes
context: Using firm-level panel data on publicly traded U.S. nonfinancial firms from 1989–2011, the paper exploits 121 staggered changes in state corporate income tax rates across U.S. states to identify tax effects on capital structure. It focuses on firms' long-term leverage, leveraging firms' headquarters' state exposure (apportionment rules, nexus) and includes industry-year fixed effects, firm controls, neighboring-state border comparisons, and treatment reversals to isolate tax-driven changes in debt policy.

It exploits index redefinitions—additions/removals that change a stock's category/habitat but not its fundamentals—using daily, weekly, and monthly return windows, matching, and calendar-time tests to infer trading-induced effects on betas, R2, and cross-category correlations.

title: Redistribution by Insurance Market Regulation: Analyzing a Ban on Gender-Based Retirement Annuities
context: This paper analyzes the U.K. retirement annuity market using individual-level data on compulsory single-life annuity purchases (age 65) from a large insurer between 1981–1998 (12,160 annuitants). The institutional setting mandates annuitization of defined-contribution pension balances; insurers price primarily by age and gender and offer a menu of contract profiles. context: This triplet refers to results from a two-country, one-good general-equilibrium model in which financial markets are complete and frictionless while goods markets are partially segmented via a proportional shipping cost. title: Has New York Become Less Competitive in Global Markets? Evaluating Foreign Listing Choices Over Time
context: This paper studies non-U.S. firms’ foreign listing choices, valuations, and capital-raising from 1990–2005, comparing New York venues (NYSE, AMEX, NASDAQ, OTC/Rule 144a) to London markets (LSE Main Market, AIM). Using firm-level panel data on listings, Tobin’s q, ownership, and country legal/institutional variables, it evaluates whether regulatory changes—notably Sarbanes–Oxley (2002) and differing U.K. listing regimes—altered firms’ propensity to list, listing premia, and post-listing financing.

title: Does the Stock Market Make Firms More Productive?
context: This study analyzes U.S. publicly listed firms over 1994–2015 (66,341 firm-year observations) to test whether greater stock price informativeness (measured by PIN, PSI and variants) causally increases firm-level total factor productivity (TFP). The unit is the publicly traded firm-year, drawing on Compustat, CRSP, TAQ, IBES and governance/competition datasets. Identification leverages within-firm panel variation and plausibly exogenous shocks—including brokerage research closures (circa 2000–2007) and S&P 500 additions—along with IV and matched DiD comparisons.

title: Should Retail Investors' Leverage Be Limited?
context: This study evaluates the January–October 2010 regulatory episode when the CFTC capped leverage for U.S. retail forex traders (50:1 for major pairs, 20:1 for others). Using account-level trade data (2009–2012) for U.S. and European retail forex traders, monthly panel data on brokerages’ capital, and high-frequency interbank quotes, it compares U.S. (treated) versus European (control) traders, brokerages, and execution prices to estimate causal effects of the leverage constraint on trading volume, returns, broker capital, and spreads.

title: Short Sale Constraints and Stock Returns
context: This paper studies U.S. NYSE stocks from 1926–1933 using monthly stock-level data (CRSP) and daily loan rates published in the Wall Street Journal’s centralized NYSE “loan crowd” list. context: This triplet concerns county-level nonfarm median contract rents in U.S. counties measured across 1930–1940, examining the effect of HOLC loans per capita (mortgage purchases/refinancings made 1933–1936). context: This paper examines how pre-crisis shareholder protection shapes firms' responses to systemic banking crises across 36 countries from 1990–2011. title: How Costly is Financial (not Economic) Distress? Evidence from Highly Leveraged Transactions that Became Distressed
context: Analyzes U.S. highly leveraged transactions (HLTs)—primarily management buyouts and leveraged recapitalizations completed in the 1980s (mostly after 1985)—following 31 firms from pre-transaction through financial distress resolution (through Dec 1995). Unit of observation is the firm; data come from SEC filings, CRSP, COMPUSTAT and Value Line industry benchmarks. The institutional setting emphasizes high leverage, junk bonds versus bank debt, and Chapter 11 restructurings, aiming to isolate costs of financial (not economic) distress by comparing firm outcomes to market and industry performance.

title: How Law and Institutions Shape Financial Contracts: The Case of Bank Loans
context: This paper studies how laws and institutions shape bank loan contracts using a global sample of syndicated bank loans originated 1994–mid-2003 to large corporate borrowers in about 60 countries (excluding the U.S.). title: 'Lucas' In The Laboratory
context: Laboratory asset-market experiments run in multiple sessions at Caltech, UCLA and the University of Utah, with human subjects trading two long‑lived assets (a risky “Tree” with i.i.d. High/Low dividends and a consol Bond) under a controlled infinite‑horizon design (random termination, continuation probability β=5/6). Subjects received time‑varying private income, faced perishable cash except in the terminal period, and traded via an electronic continuous double‑auction (Flex‑E‑Markets). The study tests causal implications of the Lucas asset‑pricing model for prices, returns and consumption smoothing.

title: Owner Incentives and Performance in Healthcare: Private Equity Investment in Nursing Homes

It matches 128 PE buyout deals (2004–2015) to ~1,674 facilities and 4.2 million Medicare patients, exploiting institutional features—Medicare/Medicaid per-diem payments, CMS Five Star ratings, and high government subsidy—to examine outcomes (90-day mortality, spending, staffing, quality) in the U.S. long-term care sector.

title: The Effect of Providing Peer Information on Retirement Savings Decisions
context: A randomized field experiment conducted in 2008 at a large U.S. manufacturing firm (~15,000 employees) evaluates how disseminating age‑bracket peer 401(k) information affects individual savings choices. The design exploits variation in union status, automatic‑enrollment defaults, and employer match thresholds to assess enrollment and contribution responses.

title: Decision Fatigue and Heuristic Analyst Forecasts
context: This paper studies sell-side analyst forecasting in U.S. equity markets using I/B/E/S forecast records for 2002–2015. Units are analyst–firm one-year-ahead EPS forecasts issued intra-day (9:00–19:00); the key institutional setting is sequential same-day forecast issuance by analysts who cover multiple firms. The authors measure decision fatigue as the number/order of an analyst’s forecasts per day and analyze effects on forecast accuracy, herding, reissuance, rounding, and investor reactions (3-day market-adjusted returns), controlling for analyst and analyst-day fixed effects and time-of-day.

context: Using U.S. microdata from the 1990s across 11 states and the District of Columbia, this analysis examines census-tract–level changes in median household income measured as the percent change from the 1990 to 2000 Censuses. The regressions relate that income change to merger-predicted changes in local bank concentration (∆BankHI1992:1999), constructed from mergers of large, non‑failing banks (≥ $1bn) during 1992–1999, with county fixed effects and local controls.

title: Information Content of Equity Analyst Reports
context: This paper analyzes 1,126 sell-side equity analyst reports issued in the U.S. during 1997–1999 by Institutional Investor All‑America analysts covering 46 industries. Units are individual analyst reports (and the covered firms’ stocks); market responses are five-day market‑adjusted cumulative abnormal returns around report release dates. The institutional setting is U.S. equity markets and brokerage‑analyst relationships (underwriting and holdings), with report content hand‑coded for earnings forecasts, summary recommendations, 12‑month price targets, valuation methods, and textual strength of arguments.

title: Industry Growth and Capital Allocation: Does Having a Market- or Bank-Based System Matter?
context: This paper studies how financial structure affects industry growth, new establishment formation, and capital allocation using a cross-industry, cross-country panel of 42 countries and 36 manufacturing industries over 1980–1990 (industry growth and establishments) and cross-country investment-elasticity estimates mainly covering 1963–95. Units are country–industry pairs and countries. The analysis exploits variation in financial structure (market vs bank size, regulatory restrictions, state ownership), overall financial development, and legal-system efficiency; industry traits come from U.S. firm data and legal origin/religion are used as instruments.

title: Volatility in an Era of Reduced Uncertainty: Lessons from Pax Britannica
context: This study uses monthly British 3% consol prices (end-of-month, Sept 1729–Dec 1959) to analyze changes in bond-market volatility across historical regimes. The unit is monthly consol returns for the UK, with comparisons to French 3% rentes. Key episodes include the Napoleonic Wars (1803–1815), Pax Britannica (1816–1913), the classical gold-standard era (1880–1913), WWI and WWII. The analysis controls for inflation, recessions, a callability premium and applies structural-break tests to link political/institutional stability to reduced financial volatility.

title: Uncertainty and Economic Activity: A Multi-Country Perspective
context: This paper analyzes quarterly country-level data on real GDP growth and realized equity-market volatility for 32 advanced and emerging economies (covering >90% of world GDP) over 1993:Q1–2011:Q2. Units are country-quarter observations. Combining a multi-country Lucas-tree model with empirical factor-augmented VARs and cross-sectional factor estimation, the study assumes a pervasive global ‘real’ growth factor and an additional common ‘financial’ factor in volatilities to separate global versus country-specific shocks and quantify their effects on growth and volatility.

title: Global retail lending in the aftermath of the US financial crisis: Distinguishing between supply and demand effects
context: This empirical study examines German retail lending using the universe of savings‑bank consumer and mortgage loan applications from July 2006–June 2008. It compares individual loan outcomes at savings banks 'affected' by their ownership/guarantees of Landesbanken hit by US subprime losses (Sachsen LB, West LB, Bayern LB) versus 'nonaffected' banks. Units are loan applications and banks; institutional features include savings banks' regional/narrow‑banking mandate and vertical support obligations to Landesbanken after the August 2007 shock.

unit_of_analysis: Units are loan applications and banks

title: Exchange Rate Variability and the Riskiness of U.S. Multinational Firms:Evidence from the Breakdown of the Bretton Woods System
context: The paper examines U.S. multinational firms around the 1973 switch from fixed to floating exchange rates, comparing two periods (pre- and post-1973) and three control firm samples. The unit is monthly stock returns of U.S. multinational firms; the study uses a single-factor market model to decompose total volatility into systematic (market risk/beta) and diversifiable components and links increased post-1973 exchange rate variability to higher stock return volatility and market risk.

title: Signaling Safety
context: U.S. publicly listed firms (NYSE, AMEX, NASDAQ) are analyzed around dividend and share-repurchase events from 1964–2013 (repurchases 1980–2013). The unit is individual firms and dividend/repurchase announcement events; the authors use firm-level monthly returns and quarterly accounting data and apply Campbell–Shiller return decompositions and VARs estimated on 60 months before/after each event. The institutional setting is corporate payout policy decisions (dividend increases/decreases, initiations, omissions, repurchase announcements), with attention to financial constraints and robustness to market-wide trends.

title: A Pyrrhic Victory? - Bank Bailouts and Sovereign Credit Risk
context: Analyzes the 2007–2010 global financial crisis with a focus on Western Europe (Eurozone and other developed countries). Units are sovereigns and large banks (bank-level and country-level observations); main data are daily/weekly credit default swap (CDS) spreads, equity returns, and country debt ratios. The institutional setting examines public bank bailouts and government guarantees (notably Ireland’s Sept 2008 guarantee and subsequent Fall 2008 bailouts), and how bailout financing (debt issuance/taxation) affected sovereign creditworthiness and fed back to bank credit risk.

title: Culture, Openness, and Finance
context: This paper analyzes cross-country variation in investor protection and financial development using 49 countries (excluding recent socialist states) in the late 20th century. Rights measures are centered on laws circa 1993, enforcement indices are averaged through the 1980s–1995, and financial/openness measures use 1985–1995 averages. The unit of observation is the country; regressions examine how culture proxies (primary religion and language), legal origin (common vs. civil law), income per capita, and measures of trade openness relate to shareholder and creditor rights and their enforcement.

title: Investor Protection and Corporate Valuation
context: This paper studies how legal protection of minority shareholders and controlling shareholders’ cash-flow ownership affect corporate valuation using a cross-sectional sample of 371 large publicly traded firms from 27 wealthy countries in the late 1990s. It exploits cross-country institutional variation in shareholder rights, disclosure rules, and enforcement regimes; units are firms nested within countries. The authors develop a theoretical model and test it with firm-level regressions relating market valuation (Tobin’s q/market‑to‑book) to investor-protection indices and governance controls.

title: Pay Now or Pay Later?: The Economics within the Private Equity Partnership
context: This paper studies 717 private equity funds (and ~6,300 investment professionals) largely vintage 2000–2015 (median 2007), using proprietary due-diligence data from a large institutional limited partner across the U.S., Canada, Europe and other regions. It analyzes how carried-interest and management-company ownership allocations (and founder status and inequality) influence senior and junior partner departures, and how those departures affect subsequent fundraising (next fund size), using regressions that control for fund characteristics and prior fund/partner performance, with outcomes observed through end-2015.

Units of observation include aggregate quarterly macro series (output gap, inflation, Federal Funds rate) and event-day asset prices (S&P 500, 10-year nominal and TIPS yields/breakeven). The institutional setting is U.S. Federal Reserve policy announcements that reveal short-term rate surprises and longer-run inflation news; estimation uses SMM, VAR moments, and event-window regressions.

title: The Market for Catastrophe Risk:  A Clinical Examination
context: This paper examines U.S. catastrophe (cat) insurance and reinsurance markets from 1970s–1990s with focus on 1992 (Hurricane Andrew) and 1996–1999 transactions (USAA’s Residential Re and CEA’s 1996 deal). Units are insurers, reinsurance treaties, and special-purpose noteholders; analyses use firm- and industry-level reinsurance contracts and premiums, AIR catastrophe-model outputs, and market outcomes across states (notably Florida and California) and Bermuda-based reinsurers. Institutional background includes regulatory guarantees, reinsurance market structure, cat-bond securitization, and post-event pricing cycles.

title: The Diversification Discount: Cash Flows vs. Returns
context: This paper examines U.S. publicly traded firms over 1979–1997 using Compustat segment data to compare multi‑segment (diversified) firms with portfolios of matched single‑segment firms (matched by 2/3/4‑digit SIC, excluding financial firms and sales <$20m). Units are firm‑years and matched portfolios; outcomes are market‑book (Q), market‑sales (M), and subsequent total returns (equity plus proxy bond returns). The study uses cross‑sectional and time‑series variation in the U.S. corporate sector to decompose the diversification discount into cash‑flow versus expected‑return components.

title: Trade Credit: Theories and Evidence
context: Using the 1988–89 National Survey of Small Business Finance (financial year 1987 with some 1986 comparisons) the paper studies U.S. non-financial, non-farm small firms (N≈3,404, stratified by region, MSA status and size up to 500 employees) across industries. It analyzes cross-sectional relationships between firms’ accounts receivable/payable, credit purchases, and measures of access to institutional finance to infer how suppliers provide trade credit and how firms demand it, exploiting industry proxies and the survey’s firm-level financing, sales, and balance-sheet details.

title: Financial Literacy and Stock Market Participation
context: Using 2005–2006 modules of the DNB Household Survey, this paper studies Dutch households (respondents aged ~22–90) to assess how financial literacy influences household stock-market participation. It links basic and advanced literacy measures to stock ownership, exploits variation in early-life exposure to economics education (schooling cohorts who attended school before the 1990s stock-market expansion) as an instrument, and leverages institutional context (Euro conversion in 2002, 2005 health‑insurance reform) to address cognition and endogeneity concerns.

title: Dividend Taxes and Share Prices: Evidence from Real Estate Investment Trusts
context: This empirical study analyzes US equity Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) from 1992–1999 (393 firm-year observations for 85 firms). It exploits REIT institutional features: exemption from corporate income tax, a requirement to distribute most taxable income as dividends (limited dividend discretion), and transparent analyst NAV appraisals. The authors regress REIT market equity values on market NAV and proxies for inside tax basis (book values) to test whether shareholder-level dividend tax benefits are capitalized into share prices.

title: Individual Investor Mutual-Fund Flows
context: This paper examines individual U.S. investors’ mutual-fund trading using detailed brokerage transactions for ~78,000 households from January 1991–November 1996 (over 325,000 purchases across ~1,100 funds). Units are household-level fund purchases/sales and monthly fund-level inflows/outflows, with holdings split into taxable and tax-deferred accounts. Fund characteristics come from CRSP and Morningstar. The institutional setting includes U.S. realization-based capital-gains taxation, fund expense ratios and loads, allowing within-investor and within-fund comparisons to identify tax-motivated trading and cost sensitivities.

title: Indirect Incentives of Hedge Fund Managers
context: This paper studies how hedge fund performance drives investor flows and managers’ lifetime compensation using a panel of 2,687 open-end hedge funds from the TASS database over 1995–2010, analyzed at monthly, quarterly and annual frequencies. The unit is fund-period observations (fund-year/quarter/month) in the hedge-fund industry, with rich contractual detail (management and incentive fees, high-water marks), fund age, and strategy (capacity-constrained vs scalable). The authors combine flow regressions with contingent-claims valuation to quantify direct and indirect pay-for-performance.

title: Appearing and Disappearing Dividends: The Link to Catering Incentives
context: Analyzes U.S. publicly traded nonfinancial, nonutility firms in Compustat at the firm-year level over 1963–2000, studying the propensity to pay dividends and a market "dividend premium" (value‑weighted log difference in payers’ vs nonpayers’ market‑to‑book). The paper links time-varying investor demand and policy episodes (notably Nixon’s 1971–74 dividend controls) to aggregate and firm-level dividend initiation and omission dynamics in the U.S. equity market.

region_bucket: U.S. publicly traded nonfinancial, nonutility firms

title: Executive Pay and Performance: Evidence from the U.S. Banking Industry
context: This paper analyzes U.S. commercial banks in the 1980s (roughly 1980–1989/1981–1990) using a panel of 147 publicly traded banks across 36 states and DC. It exploits staggered state-level interstate banking deregulation (changes in state laws permitting out-of-state entry) to study how increased corporate-control market competitiveness affected CEO pay levels, pay structure (equity/options), and turnover. Units of observation are banks and bank CEOs; identification leverages cross-state and over-time variation in regulatory regimes with bank- and CEO-fixed effects and year controls.

unit_of_analysis: Units of observation are banks and bank CEOs

title: Caught On Tape: Institutional Order Flow and Stock Returns
context: This empirical study uses U.S. equity transaction and holdings data from 1993–2000 (TAQ trades, CRSP securities, and Spectrum/13‑F institutional filings) for NYSE and AMEX-listed common stocks to infer daily institutional flows from quarterly 13‑F reports. Units of observation include stocks (quarterly and daily), individual trades, and institutional holdings. The analysis exploits trade-size patterns, panel controls, and time-series dynamics to link inferred institutional buy/sell flows to short‑term return movements and liquidity provision by non‑13F market participants.

title: Dating the Integration of World Equity Markets
context: This paper studies capital‑market liberalizations in 20 emerging markets (monthly financial series and some annual indicators through the 1980s–mid‑1990s). title: Advance Refundings of Municipal Bonds
context: This paper analyzes US municipal bond advance-refundings over 1996–2009 (MSRB/Bloomberg trades), covering 148,961 CUSIPs (~$454 billion par, ~51% of traded pre-refunded bonds). Unit of observation: CUSIP-level bonds and issuer "deals" across the 50 U.S. states. Using institutional details of municipal call provisions, SLGS escrow rules, and balanced-budget restrictions, the authors model option values (Vasicek/Hull-White), quantify option value and fees lost to issuers, and relate cross-sectional variation (e.g., state governance) to losses.

title: Learning about CEO Ability and Stock Return Volatility
context: This paper examines market learning about CEO ability using 1,873 CEO turnovers at 1,582 U.S. publicly traded firms between 1992 and 2006 (volatility data through 2009). The unit of analysis is firm-months and firm-CEO pairs, using monthly realized, idiosyncratic, and option-implied volatilities. A theoretical Bayesian learning model (dividend/earnings GBM, efficient markets) motivates tests; empirical analyses compare exogenous (death/retirement) vs forced turnovers and control for post-turnover actions, firm characteristics, and calendar effects.

title: Heterogeneous Information Arrivals and Return Volatility Dynamics: Uncovering the Long-Run in High Frequency Returns
context: This paper analyzes one year (Oct 1, 1992–Sep 29, 1993) of high-frequency intraday five-minute Deutschemark–U.S. dollar exchange-rate returns (74,880 observations) constructed from Reuters interbank quotes. The unit is five-minute return intervals in the global FX interbank market, characterized by strong intraday seasonalities. Combining a theoretical mixture-of-distributions model with empirical spectral and time-series analysis, the authors estimate long-memory (fractional integration) in volatility and identify causal impacts of scheduled macroeconomic announcements (e.g., U.S. Employment, Bundesbank meetings, Durable Goods, Inventories) on volatility.

title: Is a VC Partnership Greater than the Sum of its Partners?
context: Using VentureSource and hand-collected data on US venture-backed financings from 1987–2012 (firms founded through 2005 for exit observation), the paper studies individual venture capital partners’ board-seat investments and firm exits (IPO, M&A, exit valuation). title: Using Social Media to Identify the Effects of Congressional Viewpoints on Asset Prices
context: This triplet concerns policy-revealing congressional tweets (about 43% of the ~11K tweets from January 2013–December 2020) that reference 454 Russell 3000 firms.
