The paper finds stock-price responses are substantially larger for policy-revealing tweets, particularly those from committee members on meeting days or whose text closely matches meeting topics, consistent with revealing future legislative action.

title: Margin-Based Asset Pricing and Deviations from the Law of One Price
context: In the paper’s theoretical continuous-time general-equilibrium model, heterogeneous agents (relatively risk-averse and risk-tolerant investors) trade risky securities that have security-specific margin (haircut) requirements. title: FX Trading and Exchange Rate Dynamics

title: Private Benefits of Control: An International Comparison
context: Using 412 firm-level control transactions from 1990–2000 across 39 countries, this paper estimates the value of private benefits of control (block-premia) and links those country-level measures to financial development outcomes. title: Do Institutional Investors Destabilize Stock Prices? Evidence on Herding and Feedback Trading
context: This empirical study analyzes quarterly end-of-quarter holdings of 769 U.S. all-equity pension funds (aggregated to 341 money managers) from 1985:Q1 through 1989:Q4 across NYSE, AMEX and OTC equities. The institutional setting is U.S. mutual funds with periodic SEC disclosures, fee structures, and flow-performance incentives; analyses exploit cross-sectional and time-series variation in fund characteristics (expenses, size, age, family) and holdings to assess agency-driven risk shifting versus timing ability.

title: Do Institutional Investors Destabilize Stock Prices? Evidence on Herding and Feedback Trading
context: This empirical study analyzes quarterly end-of-quarter holdings of 769 U.S. all-equity pension funds (aggregated to 341 money managers) from 1985:Q1 through 1989:Q4 across NYSE, AMEX and OTC equities. Using SEI portfolio data ($124bn under management at end-1989), it examines stock-quarter level institutional buys/sells to measure herding and positive-feedback (trend-chasing) behavior, compares patterns across firm size and past performance, and relates institutional excess demand to contemporaneous size-adjusted abnormal returns to assess price destabilization.

title: The Twilight Zone: OTC Regulatory Regimes and Market Quality
context: This paper studies U.S. over-the-counter (OTC) equity markets from January 2001 through October 2010 using a panel of ≈10,583 domestic firms (firm-months ≈955,716). It exploits variation across OTC trading venues (NASDAQ Bulletin Board, OTC Pink tiers, Grey Market), firms’ SEC filing status, inclusion in securities manuals, and state “blue sky” merit-review regimes — including several Pink Sheets regulatory changes — to estimate effects on market quality (liquidity and crash risk). Identification leverages within-firm venue and reporting-status transitions, regulatory reforms, and cross-state institutional differences.

title: Tracing the International Transmission of a Crisis Through Multinational Firms
context: This paper studies transmission of a 2008–09 credit‑supply shock—Commerzbank’s lending cut during the global financial crisis—across German multinational parents and their international affiliates using firm‑level data (MiDi, Ustan) from roughly 2002–2015. Units are German parent firms and their foreign affiliates located worldwide. The identification exploits pre‑crisis parent reliance on Commerzbank within Germany’s relationship‑banking system to isolate an exogenous credit shock to parents and examines internal capital flows, affiliate sales and employment, and a contrasting non‑financial 2013 flood shock.

title: CEO Turnover and Relative Performance Evaluation
context: This paper analyzes CEO turnover decisions for U.S. publicly listed firms from 1993–2001 using 2,548 firms (15,798 firm‑year observations) in the ExecuComp/CRSP/Compustat samples. title: World Markets for Mergers and Acquisitions
context: This empirical study analyzes 56,978 cross-border mergers announced worldwide between 1990 and 2007, using firm-, deal-, and country-level data on completed M&A, stock-market indices, and real exchange rates. title: Do After-Tax Returns Affect Mutual Fund Inflows?
context: This paper studies U.S. domestic equity mutual funds using fund-year data primarily from 1993–1998 (≈5,866 fund-years), with a smaller 1994–1998 sample for gross inflow/outflow analysis and an institutional-fund subsample. The institutional setting is the U.S. personal income tax system: authors construct after-tax (buy-and-hold) returns for hypothetical upper-income investors (assumed dividend/short-term tax 31%, long-term gains 20%, 10% effective accrual tax on unrealized gains) and analyze how after-tax returns and capital-gains overhangs predict fund inflows and redemptions.

title: The Credit Supply Channel of Monetary Policy Tightening and its Distributional Impacts
context: This paper studies how the 2021–2022 U.S. monetary tightening—when the Federal Reserve raised short-term rates and mortgage rates jumped from roughly 3% to about 7%—affected purchase mortgage originations, house prices, and consumption across U.S. metropolitan statistical areas. title: How Management Risk Affects Corporate Debt
context: This paper studies how uncertainty about management affects corporate default risk and debt pricing using U.S. publicly-traded firms (primarily S&P1500) over 1987–2012 (CDS data 2001–2012). context: This paper evaluates whether aggregating investment-return disclosures affects portfolio risk-taking using randomized experiments. title: Marketing Mutual Funds
context: This paper studies the U.S. mutual fund industry using CRSP and Morningstar data on 2,285 diversified domestic equity funds (27,621 fund-year observations) spanning 1964–2015, with many structural estimations focused on 2001–2015. Units are fund-year observations and heterogeneous retail investors who sequentially search. It models fund choices of expense ratios and 12b-1/distribution marketing fees (capped at 1% by the SEC), decreasing returns to scale, and Bayesian learning about unobserved manager skill to quantify how marketing and search frictions shape flows, prices, and welfare.

title: Short Sale Constraints and Stock Returns

The institutional setting features a public, centralized stock-loan market where loan/rebate rates reflect shorting costs; notable regime shifts (October 1930 anti-shorting pressure, March 1932 lending restrictions) affected supply and demand for lendable shares. title: Global Financial Markets and the Risk Premium on U.S. Equity
context: This paper empirically examines daily excess returns on the U.S. market (S&P 500) and foreign equity indices—Japan's Nikkei 225 and Morgan Stanley Japan and EAFE—over 1978–1989 (1980–1989 for some indices) to test whether global market integration determines the U.S. equity risk premium. Using market-capitalization weights from Morgan Stanley, the authors model index-level daily returns under ICAPM/CAPM assumptions, account for non-synchronous trading and exchange-rate conversion, and estimate bivariate GARCH-in-mean dynamics relating conditional covariances/variances to expected excess returns.

context: The paper constructs cross-country measures of legal protection against corporate self-dealing based on Lex Mundi law-firm responses about laws in force in May 2003 for 72 countries (covering most global market capitalization). context: For this causal claim the paper focuses on U.S. quarterly aggregate consumption growth as the priced variable (quarterly data, 1952–2011) and defines “long‑run” shocks as cycles longer than the business cycle. title: The Economics of “Buy Now, Pay Later”: A Merchant’s Perspective
context: This paper studies the merchant economics of Buy-Now-Pay-Later (BNPL) using microdata from a German e-commerce furniture retailer. It combines an early-2022 randomized controlled trial that randomized display of a pay-in-30 BNPL option at checkout (~75k customers) with 2021 observational data on ~595k BNPL applications (customer credit scores, cart margins, approvals, defaults) and county-level panels (2016–2019) of checkouts to assess default cyclicality. Units include customers, shopping carts/transactions, BNPL applications, and counties; BNPL is offered in-house and subsidized by the merchant.

title: The Pass-Through of Uncertainty Shocks to Households
context: This empirical study uses employer-employee matched credit-bureau and stock-market data for U.S. public firms and their workers to estimate how firm-level idiosyncratic uncertainty affects household income, consumption, and credit outcomes. title: How is Macro News Transmitted to Exchange Rates?
context: This paper empirically examines how macroeconomic news affects the DM/USD exchange rate by transmitting through order flow, using tick-by-tick transactions (marketmaker-to-marketmaker trades) and five-minute intervals from the Reuters Dealing 2000-1 system over May–August 1996. context: This paper studies U.S. multinational corporations using Compustat panel data from 1982–2004 and detailed BEA affiliate benchmark data (1982, 1989, 1994, 1999). The units are U.S. parent firms and their foreign affiliates. The institutional setting is the U.S. worldwide tax system—allowing deferral of U.S. tax on incorporated foreign earnings (with Subpart F and marginal/statutory rate considerations)—so repatriation tax burdens vary with host-country tax rates. The authors exploit cross-firm and within-firm (affiliate) variation to link repatriation tax costs to consolidated, domestic, and foreign cash holdings.

title: Conditional Asset Allocation in Emerging Markets
context: This paper analyzes monthly equity returns for 41 country markets (21 developed, 20 emerging) from 1976–1992 with out-of-sample portfolio evaluation over 1980.12–1992.06. The unit is country-level monthly returns in US dollars. Using 60-month moving windows, the author estimates country-specific predictive time-series regressions with global and local financial predictors (lagged world and country returns, dividend yields, FX, eurodollar rate), constructs average conditional covariances, and tests mean–variance portfolio allocations (no short sales, optional 20% emerging-market cap) to assess whether emerging-market predictability and low correlations enhance global portfolio performance.

title: The Economics of “Buy Now, Pay Later”: A Merchant’s Perspective
context: This paper studies the merchant economics of Buy-Now-Pay-Later (BNPL) using microdata from a German e-commerce furniture retailer. Units include customers, shopping carts/transactions, BNPL applications, and counties; BNPL is offered in-house and subsidized by the merchant.

title: Volatility Risk Pass-through
context: This paper studies international propagation of output volatility shocks across 17 major industrialized countries (G7 plus ten others) using quarterly per-capita real consumption and output data from 1971:Q1–2013:Q4 (OECD/FRED). The setting is an open-economy macro-finance environment with substantial financial integration; the authors extract time-varying volatilities with Bayesian state-space methods and estimate pooled VARs to trace spillovers to consumption, net exports, equity-return and exchange-rate volatilities.

title: The Twilight Zone: OTC Regulatory Regimes and Market Quality
context: This paper studies U.S. over-the-counter (OTC) equity markets from January 2001 through October 2010 using a panel of ≈10,583 domestic firms (firm-months ≈955,716). It exploits variation across OTC trading venues (NASDAQ Bulletin Board, OTC Pink tiers, Grey Market), firms’ SEC filing status, inclusion in securities manuals, and state “blue sky” merit-review regimes — including several Pink Sheets regulatory changes — to estimate effects on market quality (liquidity and crash risk). Identification leverages within-firm venue and reporting-status transitions, regulatory reforms, and cross-state institutional differences.

The institutional setting involved government-sanctioned employee offers—four plan types varying discounts, subsidized financing/leverage, holding periods, tax treatment, and downside protection—with eligibility rules, salary-linked investment caps, rationing potential, and differentiation between current employees, former employees, and retirees. Units include customers, shopping carts/transactions, BNPL applications, and counties; BNPL is offered in-house and subsidized by the merchant.

title: The Credit Supply Channel of Monetary Policy Tightening and its Distributional Impacts

treatment: Reduction in dividend and long-term capital gains taxes
context: This paper studies US actively-managed domestic equity mutual funds over 1997–2006 (6,811 fund-year observations across 1,348 funds), using fund-year and quarterly fund-position observations. It links fund tax clienteles—measured as the share of assets in defined-contribution (DC) retirement plans from Pensions & Investments—to fund distributions, holdings, and performance (CRSP and Thomson holdings). The institutional setting includes pass-through mutual fund taxation and the 2003 JGTRRA dividend/capital-gains tax cuts, which the authors exploit as a policy shock to examine how manager behavior responds to changes in investor tax status.

title: Securitization and the Declining Impact of Bank Finance on Loan Supply: Evidence from Mortgage Acceptance Rates
treatment: Increasing depth of mortgage secondary market
outcome: Impact of local funding shocks on credit supply
context: This empirical study analyzes U.S. mortgage applications merged with bank Call Report data from 1992–2003. The units are individual mortgage applications and bank-year observations in the U.S. residential mortgage market, where GSEs (Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac) purchase only non-jumbo loans, creating a discrete, annually indexed jumbo-loan cutoff that sharply reduces liquidity. The paper examines how banks’ funding costs, balance-sheet liquidity, and broader securitization depth affect acceptance rates for jumbo versus non-jumbo mortgages.

title: How Do Retirees Value Life Annuities? Evidence from Public Employees
context: This paper studies 32,060 retirements in the Oregon Public Employees Retirement System (PERS) from January 1990 to June 2002. Each retiree (ages roughly 50–70) chose between a total life annuity and a lump-sum; PERS computes benefits using DC, DB, and DCDB formulas with fixed conversion factors, making PERS annuities substantially more generous than private TIAA annuities. The institutional setting includes 6% employee contributions, regular/variable account investments, no default payout, and time-series variation from 10-year Treasury yields and equity-market shocks (S&P, NASDAQ, VIX).

title: Portfolio Choice and Health Status
context: The paper uses four waves (1992, 1994, 1996, 1998) of the U.S. Health and Retirement Study, following cohorts born 1931–1941 (primary respondents aged ~51–61 at baseline). The unit of analysis is individuals and married households; the HRS provides detailed financial-asset holdings (checking/savings, CDs/T-bills, bonds, stocks/mutual funds, IRAs/Keoghs) and self-reported health. Working in the U.S. institutional setting where older households hold substantial wealth, the authors exploit panel variation in health while controlling for wealth, income and demographics to study health’s effect on portfolio composition.

title: What do we learn from stock price reactions to China’s first announcement of anti-corruption reforms?
context: The paper analyzes stock-market reactions to China’s December 4, 2012 “Eight‑Point Policy” anti‑corruption announcement. Using 2012–2013 event windows (3- and 5-day) for 2,260 firms listed on the Shanghai and Shenzhen exchanges and 81 mainland H‑shares in Hong Kong, the authors relate abnormal returns to firm-level 2011 characteristics (SOE status, entertainment & travel costs) and province-level Marketization indices to infer how curbing corruption differentially affects firms across institutional environments.

title: Investor Protection and Corporate Valuation
treatment: Cash flow ownership of controlling shareholder
context: This paper studies how legal protection of minority shareholders and controlling shareholders’ cash-flow ownership affect corporate valuation using a cross-sectional sample of 371 large publicly traded firms from 27 wealthy countries in the late 1990s. It exploits cross-country institutional variation in shareholder rights, disclosure rules, and enforcement regimes; units are firms nested within countries. The authors develop a theoretical model and test it with firm-level regressions relating market valuation (Tobin’s q/market‑to‑book) to investor-protection indices and governance controls.

title: Predatory Lending and the Subprime Crisis
context: This triplet concerns the effect of Illinois’ HB4050 anti‑predatory mortgage counseling pilot (implemented Sep 2006–Jan 2007) on 18‑month default rates specifically for subprime borrowers. The analysis uses loan‑level HMDA and CoreLogic LoanPerformance data for 2005–2007, comparing ten predominantly minority South‑Side Chicago zip codes (treated) to comparable Chicago zip codes. Units are loans/borrowers (and lenders/zip codes). HB4050 required HUD‑certified counseling for low‑FICO (≤620) or risky‑product applicants from state‑licensed lenders and imposed enforcement penalties, creating a localized shock to lending practices.

title: Regulatory Arbitrage or Random Errors? Implications of Race Prediction Algorithms in Fair Lending Analysis
outcome: Share of loans to Black borrowers
context: This triplet comes from a simulated counterfactual in which regulators switch from BISG-based evaluations to regulation using self-identified race — operationalized in the paper by using image-based race (from LinkedIn photos) as a proxy for actual race. The exercise uses U.S. small-business lending data (Lendio applications, 2017–2019, and PPP first-draw loans, Apr 2020–Feb 2021) at the applicant/borrower and lender level and finds higher shares of loans to actual Black borrowers but a reallocation toward more affluent, better-educated areas.

title: Why do firms hold so much cash? A tax-based explanation
context: This paper studies U.S. multinational corporations using Compustat panel data from 1982–2004 and detailed BEA affiliate benchmark data (1982, 1989, 1994, 1999). The units are U.S. parent firms and their foreign affiliates. The institutional setting is the U.S. worldwide tax system—allowing deferral of U.S. tax on incorporated foreign earnings (with Subpart F and marginal/statutory rate considerations)—so repatriation tax burdens vary with host-country tax rates. The authors exploit cross-firm and within-firm (affiliate) variation to link repatriation tax costs to consolidated, domestic, and foreign cash holdings.

unit_of_analysis: The units are U.S. parent firms and their foreign affiliates.

title: Adoptive Expectations: Rising Sons in Japanese Family Firms
context: This paper studies nearly the population of Japanese non-financial firms that listed between 1949–1970 (sample ~1,367 firms) and follows firm-year data from 1962 through 2000. It examines intergenerational succession events and performance for founders, biological heirs, and non-blood (adopted/son-in-law) heirs in postwar Japan, emphasizing institutional practices of adult adoption (yōshi) and arranged marriage that shape succession. Using firm-level accounting, ownership, board, and market data, the authors assess whether variation in succession and family structure causally affects profitability, valuation, and growth.
